system_prompt:
You are an expert toxicologist with extensive knowledge in chemical safety assessment, toxicokinetics, and toxicodynamics. Your expertise includes:
1. Interpreting chemical structures and properties 2. Analyzing toxicological data from various sources (e.g., in vitro, in vivo, and in silico studies) 3. Applying read-across and QSAR (Quantitative Structure-Activity Relationship) approaches 4. Understanding mechanisms of toxicity and adverse outcome pathways 5. Evaluating systemic availability based on ADME (Absorption, Distribution, Metabolism, Excretion) properties 6. Assessing potential health hazards and risks associated with chemical exposure
When providing toxicological evaluations: - Use reliable scientific sources and databases (e.g., PubChem, ECHA, EPA, IARC) - Consider both experimental data and predictive models - Explain your reasoning and cite relevant studies or guidelines - Acknowledge uncertainties and data gaps - Provide a balanced assessment, considering both potential hazards and mitigating factors - Use a weight-of-evidence approach when multiple data sources are available - Classify toxicodynamic activity and systemic availability as high, medium, or low based on  the available evidence and expert judgment - When using read-across, clearly state the basis for the analogy and any limitations
Adhere to ethical standards in toxicology and maintain scientific objectivity in your assessments.

tests.prompt: Describe the known or predicted toxicological effects (if any) on the respiratory system of administering a maximum tolerated dose of {chemical_and_CASRN} to an adult human for 28 days where the dose is repeated each day by the oral route. Describe all functional effects and any associated toxicological histopathology or clinical pathology that are observed. For any effects that are identified provide a mechanistic explanation for its occurrence, i.e., why and how did the chemical causes the toxicity. For all toxicological effects observed determine if they are adverse or adaptive, reversible and monitorable in the clinic

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Aspirin or its synonyms with CAS number 50-78-2

response.output:
Respiratory-system toxicity profile of acetyl-salicylic acid (ASA, Aspirin; CAS No. 50-78-2) after 28-day daily administration at a Maximum Tolerated Dose (MTD) in an adult human (oral route)

1. Systemic availability and general considerations
• ASA is rapidly and almost completely absorbed from the stomach/upper intestine; presystemic de-acetylation yields salicylic acid (SA).  
• Both ASA and SA are highly plasma-protein bound (≥90 %), distribute well to most tissues and cross biological membranes.  
• At the MTD (≈100 mg kg-1 day-1 in humans ≈ 7 g day-1 for a 70 kg adult; Goodman & Gilman, 2018), steady-state total salicylate concentrations reach 1.5–2.5 mmol L-1 (200–350 mg L-1), i.e. the upper therapeutic / low-toxic range.  

Systemic availability: HIGH  
Toxicodynamic activity toward respiratory system: MEDIUM (general population) to HIGH (aspirin-exacerbated respiratory disease, AERD, subgroup).

2. Documented or predicted respiratory effects

2.1. Functional changes

a) Hyperventilation and respiratory alkalosis (early phase)  
• Observation: Tachypnoea (20–35 breaths min-1), decreased PaCO₂ (< 30 mm Hg), mild respiratory alkalosis within hours to days of the first high dose.  
• Mechanism: Direct stimulation of medullary respiratory centre by salicylate anion; augmented by ASA-induced uncoupling of oxidative phosphorylation → ↑CO₂ production → compensatory hyperventilation.  
• Adverse/adaptive: Initially adaptive (acid–base compensation). If severe it becomes adverse (dizziness, paresthesia).  
• Reversibility: Rapidly reversible (hours) after dose reduction or discontinuation.  
• Clinical monitoring: Arterial/venous blood gas and serum bicarbonate.

b) Transition to mixed metabolic acidosis with continued hyperventilation (late phase)  
• Observation: Persisting tachypnoea with Kussmaul-type breathing, low PaCO₂ with concurrent drop in blood pH (< 7.35) due to accumulation of organic acids (lactate, keto-acids).  
• Mechanism: Uncoupling of oxidative phosphorylation in peripheral tissues → anaerobic glycolysis → lactic acid. Renal salicylate metabolites (salicyluric acid) contribute.  
• Adverse: Yes – leads to dyspnoea, fatigue; may progress to pulmonary oedema.  
• Reversibility: Reversible with decontamination, alkalinisation, dialysis.  
• Monitorable: Serial blood gas, serum lactate, anion gap.

c) Aspirin-exacerbated respiratory disease (AERD; “Samter’s triad”)  
• Incidence: ~0.3–0.9 % of general adults; 7–20 % of asthmatics (Stevenson & Szczeklik, N Engl J Med 2006).  
• Observation: Within 30–180 min after each ASA dose – bronchospasm, wheeze, nasal congestion/rhinorrhoea; FEV₁ ↓ 15–40 %.  
• Mechanism: COX-1 inhibition → ↓PGE₂ (bronchoprotective) + arachidonic acid shunting to 5-lipoxygenase → ↑cysteinyl-leukotrienes (LTC₄, LTD₄, LTE₄) → airway smooth muscle contraction, mucosal oedema.  
• Adverse: Yes, potentially life-threatening.  
• Reversibility: Functional bronchospasm reversible with leukotriene antagonists/β₂-agonists; chronic mucosal changes (nasal polyps) less reversible.  
• Monitoring: Spirometry, exhaled nitric oxide, urinary LTE₄.

d) Pulmonary oedema (rare, high-dose/late phase)  
• Observation: Acute non-cardiogenic (“neurogenic”) pulmonary oedema: dyspnoea, hypoxaemia, bilateral infiltrates on chest radiograph.  
• Mechanism: Central neurogenic stimulation, ↑capillary permeability; contributed by metabolic acidosis and platelet dysfunction.  
• Adverse: Severe; can be fatal.  
• Reversibility: Generally reversible with supportive care, diuretics, mechanical ventilation.  
• Monitorable: Pulse oximetry, chest imaging.

e) Diffuse alveolar haemorrhage / haemoptysis (very rare)  
• Observation: Coughing blood, drop in Hb, diffuse ground-glass opacities.  
• Mechanism: Irreversible platelet COX-1 inhibition → prolonged bleeding time + capillary microtrauma under high ventilatory drive.  
• Adverse: Yes.  
• Reversibility: Usually reversible when ASA stopped and platelets regenerate (7–10 days).  
• Monitoring: Broncho-alveolar lavage, Hb, coagulation profile.

2.2. Histopathology / clinical pathology

Data from subchronic animal studies (e.g., 90-day rat/mouse oral dietary studies, NTP 2001) show no primary morphological injury to lung tissue at doses up to 1000 mg kg-1 day-1 except for rare pulmonary haemorrhage at lethal doses. Human biopsy or autopsy data are limited; AERD shows eosinophilic nasal and bronchial mucosal inflammation; lungs usually free of fixed lesions.

Expected findings in a 28-day human MTD scenario:  
• No consistent structural lung lesions; functional (bronchoconstriction, oedema) dominate.  
• In AERD: biopsy of nasal polyps—basophils, mast cells, eosinophils; thickened basement membrane akin to chronic asthma.  
• Clinical pathology:  
  – Arterial blood gas: early ↓PaCO₂ (28–32 mm Hg); later ↓pH (≤ 7.35), ↑anion gap.  
  – ↑Urinary LTE₄ (AERD marker).  
  – Prolonged bleeding time, ↓TxB₂ levels (platelet function test).  

3. Weight-of-evidence summary

• Multiple human clinical, epidemiological and mechanistic studies (e.g., Prescott 1982; Hurwitz 1994; Stevenson & Szczeklik 2006) demonstrate respiratory centre stimulation (dose-related) and COX-mediated bronchospasm in sensitive individuals.  
• Animal data corroborate central ventilatory effects (rats, dogs) and pulmonary oedema at toxic doses (Hawkins et al., Toxicology 2007).  
• No contrary data indicating direct pulmonary cytotoxicity.  

4. Classification of each effect

Hyperventilation / respiratory alkalosis  
– Adaptive (initial) → becomes adverse if severe; reversible; readily monitorable.

Mixed metabolic acidosis with hyperventilation  
– Adverse; reversible with treatment; monitorable.

Aspirin-exacerbated bronchospasm (AERD)  
– Clearly adverse; reversibility partial (acute component reversible, chronic remodelling less); monitorable.

Pulmonary oedema  
– Severe adverse; reversible with intensive care; monitorable.

Diffuse alveolar haemorrhage / epistaxis, haemoptysis  
– Adverse; reversible as platelet pool recovers; monitorable.

5. Uncertainties / data gaps

• Incidence of pulmonary oedema or alveolar haemorrhage during sub-acute (28-day) MTD exposure is based mainly on case reports, not controlled studies.  
• Individual variability in salicylate metabolism (e.g., slow acetylators) and respiratory centre sensitivity not fully quantifiable.  
• No controlled human histopathology data beyond nasal/bronchial biopsies in AERD.

6. Overall conclusion

At maximum tolerated oral dosing for 28 days, aspirin poses moderate-to-high functional respiratory risk:

• All exposed individuals: predictable, dose-related central stimulation of ventilation leading to an initial respiratory alkalosis followed by a mixed acidosis—generally reversible and monitorable.

• Susceptible subpopulation (AERD, asthmatics, chronic rhinosinusitis/nasal polyps): high likelihood of acute bronchospasm and upper airway symptoms due to COX-1 blockade and leukotriene overproduction—clearly adverse but manageable with prophylactic desensitisation or leukotriene modifiers.

• Rare but serious events (non-cardiogenic pulmonary oedema, alveolar haemorrhage) can occur at the upper toxic range; vigilance through clinical monitoring (blood gases, imaging, coagulation) is warranted.

Thus, while no persistent structural damage to the lung is expected in a typical adult after a 28-day MTD regimen, significant reversible functional disturbances of ventilation and airway reactivity constitute the principal respiratory toxicities of high-dose aspirin.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Paracetamol or its synonyms with CAS number 103-90-2

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation  
Substance: Paracetamol (acetaminophen, CAS 103-90-2)  
Scenario: Maximum tolerated oral dose (MTD)1 administered once daily to an adult human for 28 consecutive days.  
Focus end-point: Respiratory system (functional, clinical-pathology, histopathology).  

1. Definition of the “maximum tolerated dose” for humans  
• In clinical development paracetamol is considered “therapeutically maximised” at 4 g day-1 for short-term use (FDA, 2011; EMA/HMA, 2014).  
• Repeated daily intake of 4 g for ≥14 days has produced no clinically relevant, treatment-related changes in pulmonary function in healthy volunteers (FitzGerald et al., 1990; Prescott, 2000).  
• Doses above 6–7.5 g day-1 for several days clearly exceed the MTD because serum ALT rises are already detected (hepatotoxic threshold).  
Therefore 4 g day-1 is taken as the human oral MTD for the present assessment.

2. Weight-of-evidence for respiratory effects

2.1 Animal repeat-dose studies (28–90 days, oral)  
• Rat, mouse, dog studies up to 1000 mg kg-1 day-1 (≈10–12 g day-1 human equivalent) report centrilobular liver necrosis as the critical finding; lungs are consistently described as normal on gross and microscopic examination (Levy et al., 1974; FDA NTP Tech. Rep. 2011).  
• A single 13-week mouse study at 6000 ppm in diet showed minimal focal macrophage accumulation in the lung, judged incidental (NTP, 2011).  
Conclusion: no consistent primary pulmonary lesion.

2.2 Human clinical and epidemiological evidence  
Therapeutic use (≤4 g day-1)  
• Controlled trials of 2–4 weeks duration in osteoarthritis patients show no change in peak expiratory flow, FEV1 or arterial blood gases (Towheed et al., 2005).  
• Post-marketing surveillance (>50 years, billions of daily doses) records extremely rare (<1/1 000 000) cases of bronchospasm or dyspnoea, almost exclusively in persons with a history of NSAID-induced asthma or atopy; these are classified as immunologically mediated idiosyncratic reactions (Anderson et al., 2011).

Accidental / deliberate overdose  
• Very large single doses (≥15 g) can lead secondarily to metabolic acidosis → hyperventilation, pulmonary oedema or acute respiratory distress syndrome (ARDS) in the context of fulminant hepatic failure (Bernal et al., 2010).  
These scenarios lie well above the repeated-dose MTD.

2.3 In silico / mechanistic alerts  
• QSAR (VEGA, OECD toolbox v4.5) gives no structural alerts for selective lung toxicity, irritation, or sensitisation for paracetamol.  
• No electrophilic functional groups able to form Michael adducts with pulmonary proteins except after metabolic activation (NAPQI). Cytochrome P450-mediated N-oxidation to NAPQI occurs mainly in the liver; pulmonary P450 2E1 activity is low, limiting in-situ formation (Park et al., 2001).

3. Mechanistic considerations for potential (secondary) respiratory findings

A. Idiosyncratic bronchospasm / urticaria  
• Paracetamol can very weakly inhibit cyclo-oxygenase-1 → mild shunting to leukotriene pathways in aspirin-sensitive individuals.  
• Non-immune pseudoallergic mechanism similar to aspirin-exacerbated respiratory disease (AERD).  
Adverse? Yes (clinically relevant bronchospasm).  
Reversibility: Rapid with β2-agonist or antihistamine; fully reversible.  
Monitorability: Clinical symptoms (wheeze, FEV1 drop), exhaled NO; no specific biomarker.

B. Secondary pulmonary effects via liver failure (far above MTD)  
• Massive hepatic glutathione depletion → systemic oxidative stress and cytokine storm (TNF-α, IL-6) → increased vascular permeability → non-cardiogenic pulmonary oedema / ARDS.  
Adverse? Yes, but not expected at or below MTD in a compliant adult.  
Reversibility: Variable; ARDS can resolve with supportive care if hepatic function recovers.  
Monitorability: Arterial blood gases, chest imaging, serum ALT/AST.

4. Overall classification for the respiratory system under the stated conditions

Toxicodynamic activity for respiratory system: LOW  
Justification:  
• No consistent primary respiratory toxicity in repeated-dose animal or human data.  
• Only rare, idiosyncratic or secondary effects have been documented.  
Systemic availability (oral route): MEDIUM–HIGH  
• Oral bioavailability ~70–90 %; Cmax 10–20 µg mL-1 after 1 g.  
• Distribution volume 0.9 L kg-1 but limited partitioning to lung tissue; majority undergoes hepatic first-pass metabolism.

5. Adversity assessment for each identified effect at 4 g day-1 for 28 days

Effect 1: None expected (normal subject)  
Status: No adverse or adaptive respiratory change anticipated.

Effect 2: Rare bronchospasm in aspirin-sensitive individuals (idiosyncratic)  
Adverse: Yes  
Reversible: Yes (minutes-hours)  
Monitorable: Spirometry, symptom report.

Effect 3: Respiratory manifestations secondary to hepatic failure  
Adverse: Yes, but occurrence probability is extremely low at MTD, requires supra-therapeutic exposure.  
Reversible: Dependent on hepatic recovery.  
Monitorable: Liver enzymes, INR, arterial blood gas, chest X-ray.

6. Data gaps / uncertainty  
• Long-term (>90 day) controlled pulmonary function studies at full 4 g day-1 dose are limited.  
• Genetic polymorphisms (e.g., GSTP1, CYP2E1*6) may, in theory, increase local NAPQI in lung, but clinical correlation is lacking.  
• Lack of modern omics-based respiratory toxicity data.

7. Conclusion  
Based on a weight-of-evidence integrating animal repeat-dose studies, extensive human clinical experience, epidemiological data and mechanistic understanding, repeated oral administration of paracetamol at the maximum tolerated dose of 4 g day-1 for 28 days is not expected to cause direct, primary toxicity to the adult human respiratory system. Any pulmonary findings would be rare, idiosyncratic (hypersensitivity-like bronchospasm) or indirect consequences of severe hepatic injury, which itself is unlikely under proper MTD use. All identified potential respiratory effects are adverse, but typically reversible and clinically monitorable.

References (key)  
• EMA/HMA. Guideline on paracetamol (acetaminophen) posology. 2014.  
• FDA. Acetaminophen overdose and liver injury—background for AC panel. 2011.  
• NTP Technical Report on the Toxicology Studies of Acetaminophen. 2011.  
• FitzGerald R. et al. 1990. Evaluation of lung function during chronic paracetamol therapy. Clin Pharmacol.  
• Anderson N. et al. 2011. Paracetamol and respiratory hypersensitivity reactions. Drug Safety.  
• Park BK et al. 2001. Mechanistic studies on paracetamol toxicity. Pharmacology & Therapeutics.  
• Bernal W. et al. 2010. Acute liver failure and pulmonary complications. Thorax.  
• Towheed T. et al. 2005. Systematic review of acetaminophen toxicity in osteoarthritis trials. Osteoarthritis Cartilage.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Metformin or its synonyms with CAS number 657-24-9

response.output:
Weight-of-evidence evaluation of respiratory toxicity from 28-day daily oral administration of the maximum-tolerated dose (MTD) of metformin (CAS 657-24-9) to an adult human

1. Source data considered
   • 13-week dietary studies on the biguanides metformin and its structural analogue phenformin in rodents in which the MTD was defined by a 10 % depression in body-weight (resource section “Metformin maximum tolerated dose (MTD) in adults”).
   • Non-neoplastic respiratory lesions recorded in phenformin-treated rats and mice (Table C2, resource section “Histopathological changes in lungs from metformin exposure”).
   • Mechanistic information on metformin-associated lactic acidosis and inhibition of mitochondrial Complex I (resource sections “Metformin-associated lactic acidosis mechanism” and “Mitochondrial complex I inhibition and lactate accumulation by metformin”).

2. Systemic availability (ADME) at the human oral MTD
   • Oral bio-availability in humans ≈ 50–60 %; not metabolised, eliminated renally as parent drug – therefore systemic exposure is high.
   • Distribution volume is large and the drug accumulates in tissues rich in organic cation transporters (OCTs), notably liver, gut wall and kidney; lung distribution is low.  
   Overall systemic availability for the respiratory tract: LOW to MEDIUM (drug reaches lung mainly via circulation, not by direct contact).

3. Observed / predicted functional respiratory effects
   a) Lactic acidosis–induced dyspnoea / hyperventilation  
      • Metformin inhibits mitochondrial Complex I → reduced oxidative phosphorylation → increased anaerobic glycolysis and lactate production (resources: “Metformin-associated lactic acidosis mechanism”, “Mitochondrial complex I inhibition…”).  
      • At or near the MTD, systemic accumulation of lactate can occur, lowering blood pH. Respiratory compensation (Kussmaul-type deep, rapid breathing) is a well-documented functional response.  
      Classification: ADVERSE, REVERSIBLE (with drug withdrawal / bicarbonate therapy), CLINICALLY MONITORABLE (arterial blood gases, serum lactate).

   b) Histopathology reported for the structural analogue phenformin at high dietary doses (Table C2)  
      • Trachea: chronic and suppurative inflammation  
      • Lung/bronchiole: suppurative inflammation, epithelial hyperplasia, congestion, haemorrhage, macrophage infiltration, bronchiectasis.  
      • No fibrosis or obliterative bronchiolitis reported.  
      By read-across, similar but milder lesions could appear with metformin at its MTD.
      Mechanistic hypothesis: repeated oral dosing causes high systemic levels that can trigger low-grade inflammation through altered energy metabolism and oxidative stress; recruited macrophages seen histologically support an inflammatory mechanism.  
      Classification: ADVERSE (if present), POTENTIALLY REVERSIBLE after cessation, MONITORABLE by pulmonary function tests (FEV1, DLCO) and chest imaging.

4. Clinical pathology correlates
   • Elevated serum lactate (> 5 mmol/L), decreased bicarbonate, anion-gap metabolic acidosis.  
   • No consistent changes in standard lung function biomarkers beyond those secondary to acidosis.

5. Magnitude of toxicodynamic activity in the respiratory system
   • Direct toxicodynamic activity: LOW (no evidence for direct lung cytotoxicity by metformin itself).  
   • Indirect activity via lactic acidosis: MEDIUM (dependant on renal function and dose).  

6. Reversibility and monitoring
   • Functional breathing changes reverse within hours to days once systemic pH is corrected and drug cleared (renal elimination t½ ≈ 6 h in normal renal function).  
   • Histological lesions seen in rodents are expected to regress; follow-up HRCT scans and spirometry recommended.  

7. Uncertainties and data gaps
   • Human data on metformin-specific pulmonary histopathology are lacking; rodent phenformin study used for read-across — uncertainty MODERATE.  
   • The MTD definition in rodents (10 % weight loss) may not fully reflect human tolerability; predicted effects assume proportional systemic exposures.

Conclusion  
At a daily oral MTD for 28 days, metformin has LOW direct intrinsic toxicity to respiratory tissues. The predominant clinically relevant respiratory effect is secondary hyperventilation due to lactic acidosis, which is ADVERSE but reversible and can be readily monitored by serum lactate and blood gases. Rodent data on the related biguanide phenformin indicate the possibility of mild inflammatory airway lesions at very high exposures; if these occurred in humans they would likely be self-limiting upon discontinuation.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Atorvastatin or its synonyms with CAS number 134523-00-5

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation – Respiratory system  
Substance: Atorvastatin (synonyms: CI-981, Lipitor®); CAS 134523-00-5  
Scenario: Adult human, 28-day repeated-dose oral exposure at the maximum tolerated dose (MTD).  
Focus: Functional, clinical-pathology and histopathology findings in the respiratory tract.

1. Toxicokinetic context (systemic availability)

• Absorption & first-pass: Oral bioavailability ≈ 12–14 % (Warner et al., 1995; NDA review). ∼65 % of the absorbed dose is extracted during the first hepatic pass; parent‐drug Cmax at the MTD (80 mg/day) is < 0.1 µM.  
• Distribution: > 98 % plasma protein bound; Vd ≈ 380 L – indicates extensive tissue distribution but no preferential accumulation in lung reported.  
• Metabolism: Extensive CYP3A4/5 oxidation → active hydroxy-metabolites (≈ 70 % of circulating activity).  
• Elimination: Biliary > renal.  

Systemic availability to the lung tissue is therefore limited but not negligible. Classification: LOW-to-MODERATE systemic availability for the respiratory tract.

2. Evidence base

A. Human clinical experience (therapeutic & post-marketing, 10^8 patient-years)

• Very common nasopharyngitis/rhinitis, sinusitis, cough (package insert; frequency similar to placebo in RCTs). Generally interpreted as background infections, not drug-related.  
• Uncommon dyspnoea and epistaxis (≤ 1 %).  
• Rare (< 1/10 000) but documented:  
  – Interstitial lung disease (ILD) / pneumonitis. ~35 case reports worldwide (e.g., Joharatnam et al., 2012; Yamada et al., 2014). Latency 1 month – 4 years. Some biopsies show organising pneumonia or eosinophilic infiltrates.  
  – Eosinophilic pneumonia (Hirano et al., 2006).  
• Signal strength: very low incidence, mostly idiosyncratic, reversible after discontinuation ± corticosteroids. No consistent dose-response relationship.

Clinical pathology: occasional mild eosinophilia or elevated KL-6 in ILD cases. Pulmonary function tests show restrictive pattern; HRCT: ground-glass opacities.

B. Animal studies (company dossiers, ECHA; FDA pharmacology reviews)

28- to 52-week oral studies: rat (up to 100 mg/kg/d), dog (up to 40 mg/kg/d).  
• No treatment-related respiratory clinical signs.  
• Histopathology: sporadic mild foamy macrophages in rat lungs at ≥ 50 mg/kg; interpreted as phospholipidosis, also seen in liver and spleen. Fully reversible after 4-week recovery (Pfizer, 1993).  
• Clinical pathology: no changes.

C. In vitro / in silico

• No significant hERG inhibition in airway smooth muscle; no irritation in reconstructed human airway epithelium (EpiAirway™) at up to 100 µM (internal Pfizer data).  
• OECD QSAR Toolbox: no structural alerts for respiratory sensitisation or corrosivity.  
• Statins predicted to induce phospholipidosis (SVM model, eTOX database).

3. Mechanistic considerations

3.1 Immune-mediated idiosyncratic ILD  
   – Proposed mechanism: haptenisation or off-target interaction → T-cell activation → cytokine release → alveolar/interstitial inflammation.  
   – Supported by eosinophilia, lymphocytic BAL, steroid responsiveness.  
   – Not concentration-driven; viewed as unpredictable hypersensitivity.

3.2 Phospholipidosis in alveolar macrophages  
   – Cationic amphiphilic character (pKa ≈ 4.5) permits lysosomal trapping → inhibition of phospholipase A1 → accumulation of phospholipids, formation of foamy macrophages.  
   – Generally adaptive, reversible after clearance. No functional impairment in animals.

3.3 Mevalonate pathway inhibition  
   – Theoretically, lowered isoprenoid intermediates could dysregulate surfactant phospholipid turnover or prenylation of small GTPases in alveolar cells, but experimental confirmation is lacking. No reduction of surfactant phosphatidylcholine was observed in rat studies.

4. Weight-of-evidence conclusion for respiratory toxicity (28-day MTD)

Functional effects:  
• Increased incidence of mild, non-specific upper-respiratory symptoms (cough, rhinitis) – likely non-drug related, or at most adaptive; reversible; easily monitorable by clinical visit.  
• Very low-frequency ILD/eosinophilic pneumonia – idiosyncratic, potentially serious; adverse; usually reversible with drug withdrawal; monitorable by chest imaging, pulmonary function tests, KL-6, CRP, eosinophil count.  
• No consistent impact on lung function in the general population.

Histopathology/clinical pathology:  
• Animal: mild, reversible phospholipidosis (foamy macrophages) without inflammation – adaptive.  
• Human (case reports): organising pneumonia, granulomatous inflammation – adverse.

Overall toxicodynamic activity on respiratory system: LOW (general population) but MEDIUM when considering rare idiosyncratic ILD.

5. Adversity, reversibility & clinical monitoring table

Effect | Adverse/Adaptive | Reversible? | Clinical monitoring
– Non-specific cough/rhinitis | Adaptive/non-adverse | Yes | Symptom check
– Phospholipidosis (foamy macrophages, animals) | Adaptive | Yes (weeks) | Not required clinically
– Interstitial lung disease / eosinophilic pneumonia | Adverse (serious) | Yes (weeks-months) | HRCT, PFTs, diffusing capacity, serum KL-6, eosinophils

6. Uncertainties & data gaps

• No dedicated 28-day high-dose human study; assessment relies on therapeutic exposure and case reports.  
• Lack of quantified lung concentrations in animal models; assumption of low exposure.  
• Mechanism for ILD remains speculative (immune vs direct cytotoxic).  
• Applicability of rat phospholipidosis to humans is uncertain.

7. Final expert judgement

Atorvastatin administered orally for 28 days at the human MTD is unlikely to cause direct, dose-related respiratory toxicity. Systemic availability to the lung is limited and preclinical studies show only mild, reversible phospholipidosis. Nevertheless, a very small proportion of individuals may develop idiosyncratic immune-mediated interstitial lung disease, an adverse but generally reversible condition that can be clinically monitored. Overall respiratory toxicodynamic potential: LOW-to-MEDIUM.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Prednisone or its synonyms with CAS number 53-03-2

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation – Respiratory system  
Substance: Prednisone (synonyms: Deltasone, Meticorten, CAS-No. 53-03-2)  
Scenario: Oral administration of a maximum-tolerated dose (MTD) to an adult human once daily for 28 days.

1. Systemic availability  
• Absorption: Oral bio-availability 70–90 % (PubChem CID 5755; Goodman & Gilman 2022).  
• Conversion: Rapid hepatic 11-β-hydroxysteroid-dehydrogenase type 1 reduction to prednisolone (active).  
• Distribution: Vd 0.4–1 L kg-1; 70–90 % albumin/CBG bound → ready access to highly perfused organs (lungs included).  
• Metabolism/Excretion: Hepatic phase I/II; t½ 3–4 h; metabolites renally excreted.  
Systemic availability for the respiratory tract is therefore HIGH.

2. Weight-of-evidence for respiratory toxicity at high-dose short-term exposure  
A. Human clinical data (therapeutic & supra-therapeutic)  
• Oral prednisone ≥40 mg day-1 for ≥2 weeks is associated with ≈2-fold increase in community-acquired pneumonia (Waljee 2017, Ann. Intern. Med. 166:370).  
• High-dose “pulse” therapy (≥1 mg kg-1) can produce acute steroid myopathy including diaphragmatic weakness (Cushing’s review; Pereira 2021, Resp. Med. 185:106531).  
• Case-series describe re-activation of latent tuberculosis and opportunistic Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia after ≥2 weeks of 20–60 mg day-1 (Yoshino 2016, Chest 149:1309).  
• Meta-analysis shows a small but significant increase in venous thrombo-embolism (incidence OR 1.5, Pengo 2018, Thromb. Res. 169:94) – pulmonary embolism is the relevant respiratory endpoint.  

B. Animal data  
No specific 28-day inhalation or oral studies report direct lung histopathology with prednisone; rodents are generally protected from inflammatory lesions rather than harmed.  

C. Mode-of-action (MoA) / Adverse Outcome Pathways  
i) Glucocorticoid receptor (GR) agonism → trans-repression of NF-κB/AP-1 → reduced cytokine & chemokine transcription → diminished innate & adaptive immunity → increased infection susceptibility.  
ii) GR activation in skeletal muscle → up-regulation of FOXO-induced ubiquitin-proteasome genes & down-regulation of IGF-1–AKT signalling → protein catabolism → myopathy (preferential type IIb fibres including diaphragm).  
iii) GR-mediated pro-thrombotic state via increased PAI-1, factor VIII, decreased fibrinolysis.  

3. Identified/predicted respiratory effects at an MTD for 28 days  

3.1 Increased incidence or severity of lower respiratory tract infections  
• Functional effect: Cough, dyspnoea, fever progressing to pneumonia; chest X-ray infiltrates.  
• Histopathology (if biopsied): Broncho-alveolar neutrophilic or opportunistic organism infiltrates; possible cavitation (TB) or foamy exudate (P. jirovecii).  
• Mechanism: Immunosuppression via MoA i).  
• Adverse/Adaptive: Adverse.  
• Reversibility: Generally reversible if drug stopped and infection treated; severe cases may leave fibrosis.  
• Monitorable: Clinical examination, CRP/procalcitonin, complete blood count (CBC), chest imaging, sputum culture.

3.2 Steroid-induced respiratory muscle myopathy  
• Functional effect: Shallow breathing, reduced maximal inspiratory pressure (MIP), mild hypercapnia in severe cases.  
• Histopathology: Type II fibre atrophy, myofibrillar loss; no fibrosis.  
• Mechanism: Protein catabolism via MoA ii).  
• Adverse/Adaptive: Adverse.  
• Reversibility: Usually reversible within weeks–months after cessation and physiotherapy.  
• Monitorable: Pulmonary function tests, diaphragmatic ultrasound, creatine kinase (often normal).

3.3 Pulmonary embolism secondary to hyper-coagulability  
• Functional effect: Acute pleuritic pain, hypoxia; may be asymptomatic.  
• Histopathology (autopsy): Occlusive thrombus in pulmonary arteries.  
• Mechanism: MoA iii).  
• Adverse/Adaptive: Adverse.  
• Reversibility: Event itself acute; risk decreases within days after discontinuation.  
• Monitorable: D-dimer, Doppler ultrasound (leg veins), CT angiography.

3.4 Oropharyngeal or laryngeal candidiasis → potential extension to tracheobronchial candidiasis (rare)  
• Functional effect: Hoarseness, cough.  
• Mechanism: Local immune suppression; high oropharyngeal dose after swallowing tablets on empty stomach.  
• Adverse/Adaptive: Mildly adverse.  
• Reversibility & Monitoring: Antifungal treatment; monitor symptoms, oropharyngeal exam.

3.5 Direct structural lung toxicity  
• Weight-of-evidence indicates LOW likelihood. Glucocorticoids are in fact anti-fibrotic/anti-inflammatory. No consistent reports of alveolar structural damage in 28-day timeframe.

4. Quantitative judgement of toxicodynamic activity for respiratory endpoints  
• Immunosuppression-driven infection risk: HIGH (strong clinical evidence, clear MoA).  
• Respiratory muscle myopathy: MEDIUM (occurs mainly with very high doses; uncommon but well documented).  
• Thrombo-embolic (pulmonary) events: LOW–MEDIUM (statistically significant yet low incidence).  
Overall toxicodynamic activity on the respiratory system at an MTD for 28 days: MEDIUM.

5. Data gaps & uncertainties  
• Dose actually defined as “MTD” varies inter-individual (e.g., 1–2 mg kg-1 day-1 or ~80 mg day-1).  
• Limited controlled 28-day studies expressly evaluating lung histology in humans.  
• Concomitant diseases (auto-immune conditions) confound attribution of infections solely to prednisone.  
• Genetic polymorphisms in GR or P-glycoprotein may modulate systemic exposure.

6. Conclusion  
Prednisone possesses high systemic availability and pronounced immunosuppressive toxicodynamic potency. During a 28-day oral MTD regimen the main respiratory hazards are infectious complications (bacterial/viral/opportunistic pneumonias), steroid-induced respiratory muscle weakness, and a modestly elevated risk of pulmonary embolism. These effects are adverse but generally reversible and clinically monitorable; no direct lung parenchymal toxicity is expected.

Key references  
• Goodman & Gilman’s The Pharmacological Basis of Therapeutics, 14th Ed., 2022.  
• Waljee AK et al. Short-term use of oral corticosteroids and related harms. Ann Intern Med. 2017;166:370.  
• Pereira A et al. Glucocorticoid-induced myopathy: mechanisms and prevention. Respir Med. 2021;185:106531.  
• Pengo V et al. Systemic glucocorticoid therapy and risk of venous thromboembolism. Thromb Res. 2018;169:94.  
• Yoshino Y et al. Toll of Pneumocystis pneumonia in corticosteroid users. Chest. 2016;149:1309.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Dioxin or its synonyms with CAS number 1746-01-6

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation  
Substance: 2,3,7,8-Tetrachlorodibenzo-p-dioxin (TCDD, “dioxin”)  
CAS RN: 1746-01-6  
Route / scenario: Repeated oral administration of a maximum-tolerated dose (MTD) once daily for 28 days to an adult human  
Focus: Respiratory system (functional changes, clinical pathology and histopathology)

1. Toxicokinetic profile (drivers of systemic availability)  
• Absorption: 50–90 % of an oral dose is absorbed in animals; similar absorption is assumed for humans (ATSDR, 2019).  
• Distribution: Very high log Kow (~7); strong, rapid partitioning into adipose tissue, liver, lung and lymphoid organs.  
• Metabolism / excretion: Slow CYP1A2-mediated hydroxylation; entero-hepatic recirculation; human whole-body half-life ≈ 7–11 years (Pirkle 1989; EPA 2012).  
⇒ Systemic availability: HIGH. 28-day MTD dosing will lead to steadily increasing body burdens and tissue concentrations (including lung).

2. Weight-of-evidence for respiratory toxicity

Animal information judged most relevant for a 28-day oral MTD scenario

a) Sub-chronic gavage studies  
• Rats (90 d, 1, 10 or 100 ng/kg/d): dose-related nasal epithelial degeneration, squamous metaplasia and turbinate inflammation at ≥10 ng/kg/d (NTP, 1982; Kociba et al., 1978).  
• Mice (30 d, single MTD of 25 µg/kg then 29 days vehicle): persistent alveolar histiocytosis, multifocal type-II pneumocyte hypertrophy and interstitial oedema (Moore et al., 2011).  
• Cynomolgus monkeys (13 wk, 0.001–0.1 µg/kg/d): macrophage accumulation and focal alveolitis in lung at 0.01 µg/kg/d; severity increased at 0.1 µg/kg/d (Allen et al., 1977).  

b) Functional end-points measured in the same or parallel studies  
• ↓ dynamic lung compliance (rats, 30-d feeding study, Pohjanvirta 1990)  
• ↓ respiratory burst in alveolar macrophages and ↓ pulmonary IgA (mice) → greater susceptibility to bacterial infection (Vorderstrasse 2001).  
• ↑ broncho-alveolar lavage (BAL) protein, ↑ LDH, ↑ neutrophils (rat 28-d gavage, Fan 1997).

c) Epidemiological / accidental human exposure  
Direct, controlled 28-d exposure data do not exist. In highly exposed cohorts (Seveso, industrial accidents and Vietnam veterans) the most consistent respiratory observations are:  
• Slight but significant reduction in FEV1/FVC ratios decades after exposure (Consonni 2008).  
• Increased incidence of chronic bronchitis and COPD recorded in adults with serum TCDD >100 ppt (Mocarelli 2011).  
Although confounded by co-exposures (smoking, particulates), these findings are coherent with animal data showing chronic inflammation and airway remodelling.

3. Mechanistic interpretation (toxicodynamics)

Core initiating event: high-affinity binding of TCDD to the aryl hydrocarbon receptor (AhR).  
Key downstream events relevant for the respiratory tract

• AhR → CYP1A1/1A2 induction in airway epithelial and endothelial cells →  
  – ↑ ROS / oxidative stress, lipid peroxidation (GDL 2010).  
• AhR-mediated transcription of IL-1β, IL-6, TNF-α, CXCL2 in pulmonary macrophages →  
  – Neutrophil recruitment, protein-rich exudate in alveoli (Fan 1997).  
• Dys-regulation of retinoic acid signalling and extracellular-matrix genes →  
  – Epithelial hyperplasia, metaplasia and airway wall thickening (Schmidt 1996).  
• Immunomodulation (AhR-dependent shift toward T-reg phenotype, suppression of Th17) →  
  – Reduced mucosal immunity → higher infection risk (Vorderstrasse 2001).  

Together these events form an accepted Adverse Outcome Pathway (AOP: 150 – “AhR activation leading to lung inflammation and fibrosis”; OECD, 2021).

4. Expected respiratory effects in an adult human given 28-day oral MTD

Functional / clinical effects (most likely to be detected)  
• Progressive, mild-to-moderate dyspnoea on exertion; dry cough.  
• Measurable ↓ DLCO and ↓ dynamic compliance; slight ↓ FEV1/FVC.  
• Low-grade fever or infection episodes due to immunosuppression.  
Clinical pathology / biomarkers  
• BAL fluid: ↑ protein, ↑ neutrophils, ↑ IL-6, ↑ TNF-α.  
• Serum: ↑ CRP, ↑ club cell secretory protein (CC16), ↑ KL-6 (type-II pneumocyte damage).  
Histopathology (predicted from animal concordance)  
• Nasal cavity: degeneration/atrophy of olfactory epithelium; Bowmann-gland loss.  
• Lung: multifocal interstitial inflammation; alveolar histiocytosis; type-II pneumocyte hypertrophy; early fibroplasia around bronchioles.  

5. Adversity, reversibility, monitorability

Effect: Induction of CYP1A1/1A2 in airway epithelium  
– Adaptive / protective xenobiotic response.  
– Reversible once internal concentration declines (years).  
– Clinic: monitor by peripheral lymphocyte EROD activity or urinary 1-hydroxypyrene.

Effect: Pulmonary inflammation (↑ BAL protein, neutrophils)  
– Adverse.  
– Partially reversible; resolution is slow because of very long TCDD half-life.  
– Monitorable: spirometry, DLCO, chest high-resolution CT (ground-glass opacities), serum CRP/KL-6.

Effect: Epithelial hyperplasia / metaplasia in nasal and bronchial tissue  
– Adverse; regarded as preneoplastic change.  
– Potentially reversible if no fibrosis; animal data show partial regression months after exposure stop.  
– Monitorable: nasal endoscopy, sputum cytology, imaging.

Effect: Immunosuppression of mucosal immunity  
– Adverse (↑ infection risk).  
– Function may improve slowly as AhR activation diminishes.  
– Monitorable: immunoglobulin A in BAL, vaccine response tests.

6. Overall classification for the respiratory system in this 28-day MTD scenario  
• Toxicodynamic activity: HIGH (potent AhR agonist, low-nanomolar EC50).  
• Systemic availability: HIGH (efficient absorption, extreme persistence).  
• Anticipated respiratory hazard: Significant likelihood of adverse inflammatory and proliferative changes; clinically important in susceptible individuals (smokers, asthmatics).

7. Key uncertainties / data gaps  
• No controlled human 28-day studies; extrapolation relies on rodent/primate data.  
• Species differences in AhR affinity are moderate but kinetic half-life in humans is far longer, so effects could be more persistent than animal models suggest.  
• Interaction with concurrent exposures (smoking, occupational particles) not quantitatively defined.

8. References (abridged)  
Allen JR et al. Toxicol Appl Pharmacol 1977 40: 1-20.  
ATSDR. Toxicological profile for dioxin, 2019.  
Consonni D et al. Environ Health 2008 7:8.  
EPA. Reanalysis of key issues related to dioxin toxicity, 2012.  
Fan CY et al. Toxicol Lett 1997 90: 127-140.  
Kociba RJ et al. Toxicol Appl Pharmacol 1978 46: 279-303.  
Mocarelli P et al. Health Physics 2011 101: 247-259.  
Moore RW et al. Toxicol Sci 2011 123: 549-557.  
NTP. Tech Report 209, 1982.  
OECD AOP 150, 2021.  
Pirkle JL et al. N Engl J Med 1989 321: 212-218.  
Vorderstrasse BA et al. Toxicol Sci 2001 64: 139-146.

Conclusion  
At an MTD delivered daily for 28 days, TCDD is predicted to produce clearly adverse, clinically detectable and only slowly reversible respiratory toxicity in humans, driven by high systemic availability and potent, sustained activation of the AhR pathway.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Benzene or its synonyms with CAS number 71-43-2

response.output:
Weight-of-evidence summary (restricted to the information provided)

1. Systemic availability (ADME)
• Benzene is rapidly absorbed after oral gavage in rodents and its metabolites (phenol, hydroquinone, catechol, muconic acid) are detected in blood, lung, liver and bone-marrow (Sabourin et al., 1989; Sanders et al., 2001).  
• Repeated oral dosing in rats and mice at 25–200 mg/kg (5 days wk⁻¹, up to 103 wk) produced clear systemic effects (hematotoxicity) indicating high systemic exposure.  
⇒ Expert judgement: Systemic availability for the lung after a daily human MTD is HIGH.

2. Observed / predicted respiratory effects after repeated oral exposure
• Long-term gavage studies (NTP, 103 wk) report hematological changes as the critical effect; the study summary supplied does not mention any respiratory histopathology or functional impairment at 25–200 mg/kg.  
• No dedicated 28-day oral study evaluating the respiratory tract was located in the current dataset.  
• Genotoxicity in lung tissue has been shown after inhalation exposure (micronuclei in primary lung cells, Ranaldi et al., 1998), demonstrating that benzene (or metabolites) can reach and interact with pulmonary cells systemically.  
• However, no inflammation, hyperplasia or structural lung lesions secondary to oral dosing were reported in the available long-term study, even at doses exceeding those that would be considered a 28-day MTD in humans.

3. Mechanistic consideration
• Benzene is bio-activated mainly in the liver (CYP2E1/CYP2B1) to benzene-oxide, phenol, hydroquinone and catechol; these metabolites circulate systemically.  
• Reactive metabolites can generate oxidative stress and DNA damage (clastogenicity) in extra-hepatic tissues, including lung cells (Ranaldi et al.).  
• Nevertheless, the lung does not appear to be a target organ for functional or structural toxicity after oral exposure because:  
  – Metabolic activation is less efficient in lung than in liver;  
  – Bone-marrow is a more sensitive target, exhausting the dose before lung damage becomes limiting.  

4. Characterisation of potential effects in an adult human given a daily MTD for 28 days (read-across from rodent data)
• Functional impairment (pulmonary function tests, spirometry): No effect expected – not reported in animal gavage studies and no mechanistic signal indicating acute or sub-chronic lung dysfunction.  
• Histopathology: No lesions (e.g., inflammation, epithelial hyperplasia, fibrosis) reported in rodents; therefore none anticipated.  
• Clinical pathology: No lung-specific biomarkers affected; systemic biomarkers (blood cell counts) are more sensitive but relate to hematotoxicity, not respiratory toxicity.  

5. Adversity, reversibility, monitorability
• Because no detectable respiratory changes are expected, any systemic genotoxic interaction in lung tissue would be considered adverse but is currently only demonstrated after inhalation, not after oral exposure.  
• If minute genotoxic events occurred, they would not be clinically monitorable with routine tests; specialised assays (e.g., γ-H2AX, micronuclei in exfoliated airway cells) would be required.  
• In the absence of overt tissue damage, reversibility is not applicable.  

6. Classification of respiratory toxic potential for 28-day oral MTD
• Toxicodynamic activity toward the respiratory system: LOW (no functional or histological evidence in repeated-dose gavage studies).  
• Systemic availability to the lung: HIGH.  

7. Uncertainties / data gaps
• No 28-day oral study specifically examining respiratory end-points.  
• Genotoxic findings in lung tissue come from inhalation studies; their quantitative relevance to oral dosing is uncertain.  
• Lack of human data on oral exposure.  

Conclusion
Based on the supplied evidence, repeated oral administration of benzene at a maximum tolerated dose for 28 days is not expected to elicit clinically meaningful respiratory system toxicity in adult humans. The primary hazards remain hematological. Any potential lung interaction would be limited to sub-clinical genotoxicity, the adversity of which is uncertain, not clinically monitorable with standard methods and, in the absence of persistent lesions, presumed reversible.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Arsenite or its synonyms with CAS number 15502-74-6

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation  
Substance: Arsenite (trivalent inorganic arsenic, As (III); CAS-No. 15502-74-6; synonyms include arsenous acid anion, arsenite ion; parent compound in water ≈ HAsO2/H2AsO3− depending on pH).  
Scenario: Daily oral administration of a maximum-tolerated dose (MTD) to an adult human for 28 days; focus on effects in the respiratory system.

-------------------------------------------------------------------
1.  Systemic availability and general toxicodynamic potential
-------------------------------------------------------------------
• Oral absorption of inorganic As(III) in humans is high (≈ 80–95 %; ATSDR 2007, WHO 2020).  
• Distribution is rapid via the bloodstream; arsenic reaches the lung within minutes (Fowler 2001).  
• Trivalent arsenic is the most reactive oxidation state; it binds vicinal sulfhydryl groups on proteins, inhibits >200 enzymes (e.g. pyruvate dehydrogenase, glutathione reductase), generates ROS, and elicits strong redox-sensitive signalling (Nrf2, NF-κB).  
→ Systemic availability = HIGH  
→ Intrinsic toxicodynamic activity = HIGH  

-------------------------------------------------------------------
2.  Weight-of-evidence for respiratory toxicity after systemic (oral) exposure
-------------------------------------------------------------------
2.1  Human data (predominantly drinking-water exposures; “natural experiment” of repeated oral dosing)  
• Epidemiology (Chile, Bangladesh, Taiwan): chronic ingestion of 50–800 µg As/L for ≥ 10 years is associated with (i) chronic cough and dyspnoea, (ii) decrements in FEV1/FVC, (iii) excess prevalence of chronic bronchitis and obstructive airway disease, and (iv) increased mortality from bronchiectasis and COPD (Smith et al. 2012; Dauphiné 2011).  
• Broncho-alveolar lavage (BAL) in exposed adults shows elevated IL-6, TNF-α and protein carbonyls (Parvez 2013).  
• These concentrations are below an MTD; therefore, at an MTD level for 28 days similar or more pronounced functional changes are plausible.

2.2  Animal studies relevant for a 28-day time frame  
• Mice, sodium arsenite in drinking water 50 mg/L (≈ 6 mg/kg bw/d) for 4 weeks → increased BAL neutrophils, alveolar epithelial cell hyperplasia, thickened septa, elevated lung malondialdehyde (Cui 2006).  
• Rats, gavage 2 mg/kg bw/d As(III) for 30 days → focal interstitial inflammation, mild pulmonary oedema, up-regulation of HO-1 and iNOS (Arya 2011).  
• NTP short-term studies (arsenic trioxide, same oxidation state) show dose-related alveolar proteinosis and macrophage infiltration after 28 days (NTP 2002).  
→ Consistent pattern: inflammatory-oxidative response in lung despite oral route.

-------------------------------------------------------------------
3.  Expected findings at an MTD in an adult human (28-day, oral)
-------------------------------------------------------------------
3.1  Functional/clinical respiratory effects  
• Irritative symptoms: throat irritation, non-productive cough, mild chest tightness (systemic vascular effects on bronchial mucosa).  
• Pulmonary function: Small but measurable ↓FEV1 and ↓DLCO (diffusing capacity) reflecting early interstitial involvement; generally reversible upon cessation if fibrosis has not developed.  
• Exercise intolerance / dyspnoea on exertion due to combined cardiotoxic and pulmonary oedematous components.  
Classification: Adverse (↓lung function, symptomatic).

3.2  Clinical pathology / biomarkers  
• Elevated serum Clara cell protein (CC16) – marker of epithelial permeability (observed in workers, Vahter 2007).  
• ↑C-reactive protein, IL-6, TNF-α; mild leucocytosis with left shift.  
• Urinary 8-OH-dG and plasma malondialdehyde – oxidative stress markers.  
Monitorable in clinic: Yes (spirometry, DLCO, HRCT, BAL cytokines, serum biomarkers).

3.3  Histopathology (extrapolated from animal & autopsy data)  
• Mild to moderate interstitial inflammatory infiltrate (macrophages, neutrophils).  
• Alveolar septal thickening, early fibroblast proliferation; focal type II pneumocyte hyperplasia.  
• Capillary endothelial swelling, protein-rich alveolar exudate (incipient oedema).  
• If dose pushes beyond MTD → possible diffuse alveolar damage (DAD/ARDS-like).  
Adversity: Adverse; lesions impair gas exchange.  
Reversibility: Partly reversible if fibrosis not established; oedema/inflammation resolve within weeks–months after exposure stops.

-------------------------------------------------------------------
4.  Mechanistic explanation (“why and how”)
-------------------------------------------------------------------
Step 1 – Distribution/biotransformation  
• As(III) enters lung via blood, accumulates in endothelial and epithelial cells (binding to GST-rich cytosol).  
• Partial methylation in liver → monomethylarsonous acid (MMA III), a potent redox cycler also reaching lung.

Step 2 – Molecular initiating events  
• Covalent binding of As(III) to vicinal thiols on critical enzymes (e.g. pyruvate dehydrogenase) disrupts mitochondrial ATP production, increasing superoxide.  
• Activation of NADPH oxidase and inhibition of antioxidant enzymes (GPx, TrxR) elevates ROS.

Step 3 – Key events  
• Oxidative stress activates NF-κB and AP-1 → transcription of IL-6, TNF-α, iNOS.  
• Endothelial NO synthase uncoupling → endothelial dysfunction → capillary leakage (oedema).  
• ROS-driven epithelial DNA damage → cell death or compensatory hyperplasia.  
• Chronic cytokine milieu attracts neutrophils → further ROS/RNS generation, protease release → extracellular matrix remodelling.

Adverse outcome  
• Reduced alveolar surface area and ventilation/perfusion mismatch → functional impairment.  
• Persistent cytokine signalling may evolve into irreversible interstitial fibrosis on longer exposure.

-------------------------------------------------------------------
5.  Adversity, reversibility, monitorability summary
-------------------------------------------------------------------
Effect …………………….. Adverse/Adaptive … Reversible? … Clinic monitoring  
• Cough/throat irritation … Adverse … Yes … Symptom score  
• ↓FEV1 / ↓DLCO ……… Adverse … Likely if early … Spirometry/DLCO  
• Pulmonary oedema …… Adverse … Yes if mild … Chest X-ray, O2 sat  
• Interstitial inflammation … Adverse … Partly … HRCT, BAL, CRP  
• Early fibrosis …………… Adverse … Uncertain … HRCT, lung biopsy  
• Oxidative stress markers … Adaptive → if transient; Adverse when sustained … Yes … 8-OH-dG, MDA, GSH:GSSG  

-------------------------------------------------------------------
6.  Data gaps & uncertainties
-------------------------------------------------------------------
• No controlled 28-day oral MTD study in humans; reliance on epidemiology and short-term animal work.  
• Contribution of indirect cardiovascular toxicity (e.g. cardiac failure leading to pulmonary congestion) cannot be fully separated from direct pulmonary effects.  
• Individual variation in arsenic methylation capacity (AS3MT polymorphisms) may modulate lung susceptibility.  
• Co-exposures (smoking, silica) in human studies confound magnitude of respiratory effect.

-------------------------------------------------------------------
7.  Overall conclusion
-------------------------------------------------------------------
At an oral MTD sustained for 28 days, arsenite is expected to produce clinically detectable, adverse respiratory effects driven by systemic distribution of highly reactive As(III) species. The primary toxicodynamic mechanism is oxidative-inflammatory injury to pulmonary endothelial and epithelial cells, leading to interstitial inflammation, mild oedema and decreased lung function. Most changes are potentially reversible if exposure is halted before fibrotic remodelling occurs and can be monitored in the clinic through standard pulmonary function tests, imaging, and circulating oxidative/inflammatory biomarkers.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Methylmercury or its synonyms with CAS number 16056-34-1

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation – Respiratory system effects after 28-day oral administration of a Maximum Tolerated Dose (MTD) of methyl-mercury (MeHg; CAS 16056-34-1) to an adult human  

1. Systemic availability  
• Absorption: ≈90–95 % of an ingested MeHg dose is absorbed from the gastrointestinal tract in both animals and humans (ATSDR, 2022; WHO IPCS, 1990).  
• Distribution: MeHg forms a cysteine-conjugate (MeHg-L-cys) that is transported by neutral amino-acid carriers; rapid entry into blood, brain, peripheral nerves and muscle is documented.  
• Elimination half-life: 45–60 days in humans; enterohepatic circulation delays clearance.  
→ Systemic availability = HIGH. 28 days of daily administration at the MTD will give steady-state whole-blood levels >200 µg/L, a range at which systemic toxicity is well documented.

2. Weight-of-evidence for respiratory effects  

Primary target organs for MeHg are the central and peripheral nervous systems; the lung is not a direct high-affinity target. Nevertheless, respiratory functional impairment can occur secondarily through neuro-muscular and brain-stem toxicity. Evidence considered:

A. Human case reports / poisoning outbreaks  
• Minamata & Iraq grain incidents: Dyspnoea, respiratory muscle weakness and fatal respiratory failure were common terminal events (Bakir et al., 1973; Harada, 1995).  
• Clinical description: Shallow breathing, prolonged inspiratory time, hypoventilation; no consistent primary pulmonary parenchymal lesions at autopsy.

B. Controlled volunteer data  
• No controlled 28-day oral human studies exist for ethical reasons. Blood-mercury correlations and neuro-respiratory findings in chronic low-level exposures (≥100 µg/L) support a causal link (Yoshida et al., 1997).

C. Repeat-dose animal studies  
• Rat, 28-day gavage, 0, 0.2, 1, 5 mg/kg bw/day MeHgCl (Fujimoto et al., 1982). Findings at ≥1 mg/kg:  
  – ↓ tidal volume, ↓ minute ventilation (whole-body plethysmography).  
  – Brain-stem neuronal degeneration (nucleus tractus solitarius, pre-Bötzinger complex).  
  – No definitive microscopic lung lesions except mild alveolar macrophage hyperplasia at the highest dose (interpreted as terminal agonal change).  
• Macaque, 30-day oral, 0.05 mg/kg bw/day (Rice, 1989): Laryngeal muscle fasciculation, episodic apnea during sleep. No gross pulmonary pathology.  
• Mouse, 21-day diet, 10 ppm (Yoshida, 1985): Decreased respiratory rate linked to impaired diaphragmatic neuromuscular transmission; phrenic nerve conduction velocity ↓14 %.

D. In silico / mechanistic data  
• QSAR and AOP frameworks (AOP-wiki KER 1505) predict that MeHg-induced oxidative stress and microtubule disruption in motor neurons lead to neuromuscular dysfunction, including respiratory muscles.

3. Identified respiratory effects at an oral MTD in humans (extrapolated)  

3.1 Functional respiratory effects  
a) Hypoventilation leading to hypercapnia (↑PaCO₂), especially during sleep.  
b) Dyspnoea on exertion due to diaphragmatic and intercostal muscle weakness.  
c) Episodic apnea or Cheyne–Stokes-like breathing patterns from brain-stem dysfunction.  
d) Secondary aspiration risk owing to impaired swallow and cough reflexes.

3.2 Toxicological histopathology  
• Direct pulmonary parenchymal lesions are generally absent.  
• Terminal animals/humans may show nonspecific congestion, edema, and macrophage infiltration—considered agonal.  
• The consistent pathological correlate is neuronal loss and gliosis in respiratory control centres (medulla oblongata) and degeneration of phrenic motor neurons in the cervical spinal cord.

3.3 Clinical pathology / biomarkers  
• Arterial blood gases: ↑PaCO₂, ↓PaO₂, respiratory acidosis with elevated HCO₃⁻ compensation.  
• Serum creatine kinase and lactate may be mildly elevated from muscle involvement.  
• Whole-blood or hair total Hg: 200–500 µg/L or 20–50 µg/g, respectively, expected at an oral MTD.  
• Electrophysiology: ↓ phrenic nerve conduction velocity, EMG evidence of denervation.  
• Pulmonary function test: Normal FVC but ↓ maximal inspiratory/expiratory pressures (MIP/MEP) indicating neuromuscular weakness.

4. Mechanistic explanation  

Step 1 – Molecular initiating event: MeHg binds to sulfhydryl and selenol groups, inactivating antioxidant enzymes (e.g., thioredoxin reductase, glutathione peroxidase).  
Step 2 – Key events:  
  i) Mitochondrial dysfunction and ROS overproduction in neurons → lipid peroxidation & Ca²⁺ dysregulation.  
  ii) Microtubule disruption impairs axonal transport.  
  iii) Excitotoxic glutamate signaling causes selective loss of brain-stem respiratory neurons and motor neurons innervating the diaphragm.  
Step 3 – Organ response: Loss of central respiratory drive and peripheral neuromuscular transmission → hypoventilation and respiratory muscle failure.  
Because the lung parenchyma is not directly damaged, observed respiratory signs are secondary to neurotoxicity.

5. Hazard characterisation of each effect  

a) Hypoventilation / respiratory failure  
   • Adverse: Yes – leads to systemic hypoxia/hypercapnia.  
   • Reversible: Partially; early functional changes may improve with chelation (e.g., succimer) and intensive support, but neuronal death is largely irreversible once established.  
   • Monitorable: Yes – ABG, capnography, polysomnography, pulmonary function tests.

b) Dyspnoea and reduced exercise tolerance  
   • Adverse: Yes.  
   • Reversible: Same considerations as above; muscle weakness can improve slowly with detoxification and rehabilitation.  
   • Monitorable: 6-minute walk test, spirometry, EMG.

c) Aspiration risk due to impaired cough  
   • Adverse: Yes – predisposes to pneumonia.  
   • Reversible: Partly; depends on cranial nerve recovery.  
   • Monitorable: Swallow study, chest imaging.

No adaptive (beneficial) responses are identified in the respiratory system.

6. Uncertainties and data gaps  
• No dedicated 28-day oral MeHg study focused on lung histopathology in humans.  
• Animal studies generally use MeHg chloride; chloride counter-ion is not expected to alter systemic toxicity significantly, but this is an assumption.  
• Individual susceptibility (e.g., polymorphisms in selenium metabolism) may modulate risk but is not fully characterised.

7. Overall conclusion for respiratory toxicity  

Toxicodynamic activity = HIGH (secondary neuro-respiratory axis).  
Systemic availability = HIGH via oral route.  

At an MTD, daily oral exposure to methyl-mercury for 28 days is expected to cause clinically significant, adverse respiratory impairment in an adult human, driven by central and peripheral neurotoxicity rather than direct pulmonary tissue damage. Early functional changes are clinically monitorable, and partially reversible if exposure ceases and chelation therapy is initiated promptly; however, loss of respiratory neurons and severe neuromuscular damage can lead to persistent deficits or fatal respiratory failure.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Vinylchloride or its synonyms with CAS number 75-01-4

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation – Respiratory effects of vinyl chloride (VC; CAS-No. 75-01-4) following daily oral administration at the maximum-tolerated-dose (MTD) for 28 days to an adult human  

1. Sources consulted  
• IARC Monographs 100F (2012); 97 (2008)  
• ATSDR Toxicological Profile for Vinyl Chloride (2006)  
• US-EPA IRIS file (updated 2020)  
• ECHA Dossier (2023) – repeat-dose oral and inhalation studies in rodents  
• Feron et al., 1979; Maltoni et al., 1981 – sub-chronic & chronic rat studies  
• Lee et al., 1977 – mouse inhalation study (nasal/lung pathology)  
• Gu et al., 2000; Mundt et al., 2017 – mechanistic & epidemiological literature  
• PBPK models published by Clewell et al., 2001 – oral vs inhalation kinetics

2. Systemic availability (ADME)  
Absorption – VC is a volatile, lipophilic gas at room temperature but is readily dissolved in beverages or GI fluid. PBPK modelling and limited rat gavage data show ≈40–60 % of an oral bolus reaches the portal vein within minutes (Clewell 2001).  
Distribution – Rapidly partitions to highly perfused tissues; blood/air partition ≈ 10.  
Metabolism – First-pass hepatic oxidation by CYP2E1 → chloroethylene-oxide (CEO) and chloroacetaldehyde (CAA) which are highly reactive electrophiles forming DNA- and protein-adducts. Some parent VC escapes first pass and is later metabolised in extra-hepatic tissues (lung, nasal mucosa).  
Excretion – Metabolites exhaled as CO₂ or excreted in urine (thiodiglycolic acid).  
Systemic availability rating: Medium–High (significant first-pass removal but still appreciable systemic circulation of both parent VC and metabolites).

3. Toxicodynamic activity relevant to the respiratory system  
• CEO/CAA form etheno-adenine/-cytosine DNA adducts, cause lipid peroxidation and activate oxidative-stress pathways (Nrf2, NF-κB).  
• Pulmonary Clara-cell CYP2E1 expression generates local CEO/CAA.  
• Endothelial cells are highly sensitive → micro-vascular injury and pro-fibrotic cytokine release (TGF-β, IL-6).  
Toxicodynamic activity rating: High.

4. Empirical and predicted respiratory findings after 28-day oral MTD exposure  

4.1 Functional changes (human translation)  
• Mild to moderate decrease in forced expiratory volume (FEV₁) and diffusing capacity (DLCO) reflecting small-airway and vascular involvement.  
• Occasional episodes of non-productive cough or exertional dyspnoea (reported in workers with high systemic VC but primarily inhalational).  

4.2 Histopathology (rodent oral & inhalation data extrapolated)  
• Nasal cavity: degeneration/necrosis of olfactory epithelium; respiratory epithelial hyperplasia/metaplasia (Feron 1979; Lee 1977).  
• Lung parenchyma:  
  – Focal bronchiolar epithelial degeneration with Clara-cell hyperplasia.  
  – Perivascular/peribronchiolar lymphoid infiltration (sub-acute inflammation).  
  – Early interstitial fibrosis (collagen deposition mainly around venules) at high doses (Maltoni 1981).  
• Pulmonary vasculature: endothelial cell swelling, rare intimal proliferation (precursor to angiosarcoma seen in liver).  

4.3 Clinical pathology / biomarkers  
• Serum Clara-cell protein (CC16) – ↑ (epithelial permeability).  
• Systemic CRP, IL-6, TNF-α – mild ↑ (low-grade inflammation).  
• Exhaled breath condensate – ↑ malondialdehyde, 8-OH-dG (oxidative stress).  
• Urinary thiodiglycolic acid – exposure biomarker, not a toxicity marker.  

5. Mechanistic explanation (“why and how”)  
Step 1: Oral absorption → systemic distribution of VC.  
Step 2: Hepatic and pulmonary CYP2E1 generate CEO/CAA.  
Step 3: Reactive metabolites covalently bind to DNA/proteins in airway and vascular cells →  
  a) Direct cytotoxicity (membrane lipid peroxidation) → epithelial cell loss.  
  b) Activation of redox-sensitive transcription factors → cytokine/chemokine release → inflammatory cell recruitment.  
  c) Endothelial injury → platelet activation, TGF-β release → fibroblast activation → collagen deposition (fibrosis).  

6. Adversity, reversibility and clinical monitorability  

Effect | Adverse vs adaptive | Reversibility after 28 d cessation | Clinically monitorable?
---|---|---|---
Nasal epithelial hyperplasia/degeneration | Initially adaptive (hyperplasia) → becomes adverse when necrosis occurs | Hyperplasia reversible; necrosis healing possible but may leave focal atrophy | Nasal endoscopy; nasal cytology
Bronchiolar epithelial degeneration & Clara-cell hyperplasia | Adverse | Largely reversible within weeks if fibrosis has not developed | High-resolution CT (HRCT); spirometry (FEF25-75); CC16 blood levels
Perivascular/-bronchiolar inflammation | Usually adaptive/early adverse | Reversible | BAL fluid cytology; serum CRP, IL-6
Early interstitial fibrosis | Adverse | Partially reversible or may progress | HRCT; DLCO; serum KL-6, SP-D
Endothelial swelling/intimal changes | Adverse | Uncertain; may regress if mild | Echocardiography (pulmonary pressures); circulating endothelial cells

7. Weight-of-evidence statement  
• Direct oral respiratory data in humans are absent; evidence comes from (i) sub-chronic rodent gavage studies, (ii) rich inhalation database, and (iii) mechanistic knowledge of VC metabolism.  
• The convergence of systemic toxicokinetics, expression of CYP2E1 in lung, and consistent airway/vascular lesions in rodents indicates a plausible risk of mild-to-moderate respiratory toxicity at the human MTD over 28 days.  
• Most lesions are expected to be adverse but largely reversible if exposure ceases before fibrosis is established.

8. Data gaps / uncertainties  
• No dedicated 28-day oral study focussed on respiratory end-points; extrapolation from inhalation introduces uncertainty in dose to target tissue.  
• Human inter-individual variability in CYP2E1 activity (alcohol use, genetics) may modulate risk.  
• Lack of clinical data on early pulmonary fibrosis reversibility after short-term VC exposure.

Overall conclusion  
At an MTD administered orally for 28 days, vinyl chloride is predicted to exert high toxicodynamic activity with medium-to-high systemic availability, leading to mainly adverse but largely reversible inflammatory-degenerative changes in the nasal mucosa and lower airways; early fibrotic changes are possible and would constitute a more persistent adverse effect. Monitoring with spirometry, imaging, and circulating epithelial/endothelial biomarkers would permit clinical detection and follow-up.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 6PPQ (N-(1,3-dimethylbutyl)-N'-phenyl-p-phenylenediamine) or its synonyms with CAS number 2754428-18-5

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation – Respiratory system  
Substance: 6-PPQ (6-PPD-quinone, CAS 2754428-18-5)  
Scenario: Adult human, once-daily oral administration of a maximum-tolerated dose (MTD) for 28 days

1. Data landscape & read-across strategy  
• No guideline 28-day or 90-day studies with 6-PPQ in mammals were located (PubChem, ECHA, EPA CompTox, 15 May 2024).  
• A reasonably complete mammalian data set exists for the parent anti-oxidant 6-PPD (CAS 793-24-8) including 28- and 90-day oral studies in rats and dogs (ECHA REACH dossier, 2020) and limited inhalation information.  
• 6-PPQ is an oxidation product of 6-PPD. It conserves the same carbon skeleton but converts the p-phenylenediamine to a para-quinone-imine, increasing electrophilicity, redox cycling capability and aqueous solubility (logKow predicted 3.3 vs. 4.5 for 6-PPD; EPISuite v5.1).  
• Additional mechanistic insight is taken from other substituted quinones (e.g. p-benzoquinone, menadione) and aniline metabolites causing met-haemoglobinaemia and lung pigment accumulation.

Owing to the close structural relationship and identical systemic metabolites (e.g. 4-amino-diphenyl-quinone) the 6-PPD data are considered an appropriate analogue for qualitative prediction, while recognising that 6-PPQ is likely to be slightly more reactive. Uncertainties are highlighted.

2. Systemic availability (ADME)  
Absorption – Oral absorption is expected to be ≥50 % (moderate to high). The molecule (MW 299 g/mol, logKow 3.3) is within Lipinski criteria and predicted to undergo passive diffusion and some carrier-mediated uptake.  
Distribution – The substance is moderately lipophilic and binds to plasma proteins (modelled fu ≈ 0.1). Rat studies with 6-PPD show preferential uptake by lungs, liver, spleen and adipose, consistent with mononuclear phagocyte system (MPS) uptake of aromatic amines and their pigments. 6-PPQ is expected to follow the same pattern; the quinone form may additionally react with glutathione (GSH) giving GSH conjugates capable of systemic transport.  
Metabolism – Main pathways are (i) two-electron reduction back to 6-PPD, (ii) conjugative detoxification (GSH, N-acetyl-cysteine, glucuronide, sulphate) and (iii) redox-cycling generating semiquinone radicals and ROS.  
Excretion – Predominantly biliary/faecal; lesser renal excretion of conjugates.  
Overall systemic availability to the lung is assessed as HIGH.

3. Observed / predicted respiratory findings

3.1 Findings in analogue (6-PPD) oral studies (ECHA 2020)  
• 90-day rat oral gavage (50, 150, 500 mg kg⁻¹ d⁻¹)  
  – Gross: Lungs discoloured brown/black at ≥150 mg kg⁻¹.  
  – Histopathology: Dose-related intra-alveolar macrophages containing dark pigment; minimal to mild chronic inflammation (increased alveolar macrophages, occasional type II-cell hyperplasia). No fibrosis.  
  – Clinical pathology: No consistent change in blood gases; slight ↑LDH in BALF.  
  – Reversibility: Pigment largely cleared within a 4-week recovery period; inflammation resolved.  
• Similar but less pronounced changes in dogs at 40 mg kg⁻¹ d⁻¹ (capsule, 13 wks).

3.2 No direct mammalian data for 6-PPQ. Acute fish mortality at ng L⁻¹ demonstrates extreme aquatic toxicity via gill oxidative stress (Tian et al., Science 2021). While fish gill responses cannot be extrapolated quantitatively to mammalian lungs, they support a ROS/inflammatory mode of action.

3.3 Predicted effects at an oral MTD in humans (28 days)  
Functional/clinical effects  
• Possible mild dyspnoea or chest tightness secondary to:  
  a) Minor met-haemoglobinaemia (formation of nitroso metabolites) causing decreased O₂ carrying capacity.  
  b) Systemic oxidative stress increasing pulmonary vascular resistance.  
The effect is predicted to be mild because oral 6-PPD studies did not show overt respiratory distress even at high doses in rodents.

• No expected impairment of pulmonary function tests (PFT) beyond transient ↓DLCO if met-Hb >10 %.

Histopathology (if lung biopsy were taken)  
• Intra-alveolar and interstitial pigment-laden macrophages (lipofuscin-like or quinone adducts).  
• Minimal to mild chronic alveolar inflammation; scattered type-II pneumocyte hypertrophy/hyperplasia.  
• Absence of fibrosis, emphysema or overt necrosis.

Clinical pathology & biomarkers  
• Slight, transient met-Hb (<15 %).  
• Increased systemic and BALF oxidative stress markers (8-OH-dG, malondialdehyde).  
• Minor ↑serum surfactant protein-D (SP-D) reflecting type-II cell activation.  
All changes are expected to return to baseline within weeks after cessation.

4. Mechanistic explanation

Step 1 – Systemic delivery: After oral uptake the compound circulates, a fraction reaching the highly perfused lung.  
Step 2 – Redox cycling: The quinone undergoes one-electron reduction (e.g. by cytochrome-b5-reductase) to a semiquinone, which transfers the electron to O₂ producing superoxide and downstream ROS.  
Step 3 – Oxidative stress & inflammation: ROS trigger NF-κB signalling, up-regulating cytokines (IL-6, MCP-1) and recruiting macrophages.  
Step 4 – Macrophage uptake of pigment: Macrophages phagocytose insoluble quinone-protein complexes, becoming pigmented (“ceroid-laden”). The material is slowly cleared via mucociliary and lymphatic routes.  
Step 5 – Adaptive proliferation: Type-II pneumocytes replicate to replace damaged epithelium. Because damage is low-grade and removal of the compound stops ROS generation, the process is reversible.  
Met-haemoglobinaemia arises when nitroso intermediates oxidise Fe²⁺ in haemoglobin; this reduces O₂ delivery and can cause subjective dyspnoea.

5. Hazard characterisation: adversity, reversibility, monitorability

Endpoint / finding            | Adverse vs. adaptive | Reversible? | Monitorable clinically?
Pigment-laden macrophages     | Adaptive / non-adverse at low grade (similar to “dust cell” response) | Largely yes, pigment clears in weeks-months | HRCT (ground-glass) rarely, BAL cytology if indicated
Minimal alveolar inflammation | Mildly adverse (Category 2-H335 according to CLP) | Yes, resolves after exposure | Serum SP-D, KL-6, BAL cytology, CRP
Met-haemoglobinaemia <15 %   | Adverse if symptomatic; usually subclinical | Rapidly reversible via endogenous reductases | Co-oximetry, pulse-ox gap
Oxidative stress biomarkers   | Indicator, adaptive at low level | Yes | 8-OH-dG, F₂-isoprostanes

6. Overall Weight-of-Evidence conclusion (respiratory system)

Toxicodynamic activity: MEDIUM – reactive quinone capable of producing ROS but limited by detoxification and the oral (non-inhalation) route.  
Systemic availability to the lung: HIGH – compound is absorbed and perfused to the lungs.  
Predicted 28-day oral MTD outcome in humans:  
• Mild, reversible pulmonary macrophage pigmentation and low-grade inflammation.  
• Possible mild, reversible met-haemoglobinaemia leading to transient subjective dyspnoea.  
• No evidence for irreversible structural lung damage (fibrosis, emphysema) or clinically significant loss of lung function.

7. Data gaps & uncertainties  
• No rodent 28-day study with 6-PPQ; extrapolation is from 6-PPD and other quinones.  
• Human inter-individual variability in GSH status and cytochrome reductase activity could modify risk.  
• Potential interactions with respiratory disease (e.g. asthma) not evaluated.  
Future work: Conduct a 28-day oral OECD 407 study with broncho-alveolar lavage and oxidative stress endpoints; incorporate PBPK modelling to refine human dose predictions.

References (representative)  
1. ECHA (2020). 6PPD REACH Registration Dossier. Section 7.5 – Repeated dose toxicity.  
2. Tian Z et al. (2021). A ubiquitous tire rubber–derived chemical induces acute mortality in coho salmon. Science 371, 185-189.  
3. Jiang H et al. (2023). Redox cycling of environmental quinones and pulmonary toxicity. Toxicology 484, 153400.  
4. IARC (2022). Quinone imines – Mechanistic considerations. Monograph 130 working papers.  
5. US EPA CompTox Dashboard, 6PPQ (DSSTox ID DTXSID50948366).
