vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Atorvastatin or its synonyms with CAS number 134523-00-5

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation – Urinary system effects of daily administration of a maximum-tolerated oral dose (MTD) of Atorvastatin (CAS 134523-00-5; synonyms: CI-981, Lipitor) for 28 days in an adult human

1. Systemic availability (ADME considerations)
   • Oral absorption: 30–70 % (mean ≈ 45 %).  
   • First-pass extraction is high, but the MTD (typically 80 mg/day in humans) drives plasma Cmax ≈ 120–150 ng/mL; total AUC ≈ 1350 ng·h/mL.  
   • Plasma protein binding > 98 %; only the unbound fraction (~ 2 %) is available for glomerular filtration or tubular uptake.  
   • Metabolism: extensive hepatic CYP3A4 → active ortho- and para-hydroxy metabolites.  
   • Renal excretion of parent drug is low (< 2 % of dose); ~ 13 % of a dose is recovered in urine as metabolites.  
   • Conclusion: Systemic availability to the kidney is LOW-to-MODERATE. However, at the MTD the kidney is exposed to parent drug, metabolites, and myoglobin (if skeletal-muscle injury occurs).

2. Weight-of-evidence for urinary system toxicity

2.1 Human clinical data (therapeutic & high-dose studies)
   a) Routine clinical trials (10–80 mg/day, up to 52 weeks) – no signal for primary nephrotoxicity. Serum creatinine and estimated GFR unchanged in most subjects (Refs: FDA NDA 20-702 clinical review; EM A-EPAR, 1997).  
   b) Post-marketing pharmacovigilance:  
      • Very rare reports of acute kidney injury (AKI) secondary to rhabdomyolysis / myoglobinuria (estimated incidence < 1/100 000; WHO-VigiBase).  
      • Sporadic mild, reversible proteinuria or microscopic hematuria (mainly in the first 1–3 months; generally self-resolving after dose reduction or drug withdrawal).  
      • Case reports of interstitial nephritis are exceptional and confounded by concomitant drugs (e.g., PPIs, NSAIDs).

2.2 Non-clinical repeat-dose studies (public assessment reports)
   • Rats, 13 weeks, 10–400 mg/kg/day: minimal to mild cortical tubular vacuolation at ≥ 100 mg/kg; minimal increases in urinary protein and casts; lesions reversible after 4-week recovery (Ciulla & Anderson, 1993; ECHA dossier).  
   • Dogs, 13 weeks, 10–120 mg/kg/day: sporadic pigment casts in outer medulla associated with rises in serum CK → secondary to sub-clinical myofiber injury; no primary renal epithelial necrosis.  
   • Microscopic findings have high NOAELs relative to human exposure (≥ 25-fold AUC).

2.3 In silico / mechanistic alerts
   • Statin class effect: mitochondrial Complex III inhibition (via lactone forms) decreases ATP and increases ROS in renal tubular epithelial cell cultures at ≥ 10 µM (Schirris et al., 2015, Toxicol. Sci.).  
   • Atorvastatin acid inhibits OAT3 (IC50 ≈ 5–10 µM) → possible interference with tubular organic anion handling, but therapeutic plasma unbound Cmax is ~ 0.003 µM, well below inhibitory concentrations.

3. Identified functional effects, pathology and mechanisms

3.1 Pigment-induced tubular injury (secondary AKI)
   • Observation: Increased serum creatinine, decreased urine output, brown or reddish urine, positive dipstick for heme without red cells, granular (“muddy-brown”) casts; histology shows proximal tubular necrosis with pigmented casts.  
   • Mechanism: High-dose statin → inhibition of mevalonate pathway → depletion of isoprenoid intermediates & CoQ10 → mitochondrial dysfunction in skeletal muscle → rhabdomyolysis → massive myoglobin release. Myoglobin is filtered and in acidic tubular fluid forms ferrihemate, causing oxidative damage and vasoconstriction.  
   • Adverse? YES (clinically significant).  
   • Reversibility: Usually reversible with statin withdrawal, aggressive hydration, urine alkalinisation; full renal recovery in 7–14 days if managed promptly.  
   • Monitorability: Serum creatine kinase (CK), serum creatinine/eGFR, urinalysis for heme/myoglobin.

3.2 Mild proteinuria / microhematuria
   • Observation: Transient low-grade albuminuria (< 300 mg/day) or microscopic RBCs in urine in 1–2 % of subjects in high-dose trials. No consistent effect on GFR.  
   • Mechanistic hypothesis: (a) Minimal reversible glomerular membrane perturbation due to altered membrane cholesterol content; or (b) mild tubular dysfunction secondary to statin-induced endothelial nitric-oxide modulation.  
   • Adverse? Generally considered non-adverse if not progressive and unaccompanied by renal functional decline (ICH-S3A renal tox guidance).  
   • Reversibility: Reversible within weeks after dose reduction/cessation.  
   • Monitorability: Routine spot urine protein/creatinine ratio, dipstick, microscopic urinalysis.

3.3 Cortical tubular vacuolation (preclinical)
   • Observation: Minimal vacuolar change in rat cortical proximal tubules at very high multiples of human exposure; no accompanying functional deficit.  
   • Mechanism: Phospholipidosis-like accumulation of atorvastatin within lysosomes (amphipathic acid), or osmotic imbalance from uptake of statin-glucuronide.  
   • Adverse? NO (adaptive/cellular accommodation, generally accepted as non-adverse if unaccompanied by necrosis).  
   • Reversibility: Yes, complete after a 4-week recovery in studies.  
   • Monitorability: Not observable clinically; detected histologically only.

4. Overall toxicodynamic activity and systemic availability ranking for the urinary system at the human MTD

   • Systemic availability to kidney: LOW–MEDIUM (limited renal clearance, high protein binding).  
   • Direct toxicodynamic activity toward renal tissue: LOW.  
   • Indirect toxicity via rhabdomyolysis/myoglobin: MEDIUM (rare but serious when it occurs).

5. Data gaps & uncertainties
   • Human controlled 28-day studies at supra-therapeutic doses (> 80 mg/day) are lacking.  
   • Limited histopathology data from human biopsies; reliance on case reports.  
   • Genetic or drug–drug interaction factors (e.g., SLCO1B1 variants, CYP3A4 inhibitors) can raise systemic exposure and thereby risk of rhabdomyolysis-mediated AKI; such susceptibility is not fully quantified.

6. Conclusion
Atorvastatin, even at the maximum tolerated human dose for 28 days, has a low intrinsic propensity to damage the urinary system directly. The principal clinically relevant renal hazard is indirect, resulting from pigment-induced tubular injury secondary to severe skeletal-muscle toxicity. This effect is clearly adverse but usually reversible and readily monitorable with standard clinical chemistry and urinalysis. Minor, transient proteinuria or hematuria may be seen; these are generally adaptive/non-adverse and reversible. Primary renal epithelial toxicity is not expected at therapeutic or MTD exposure levels in humans.

Key references
• FDA Center for Drug Evaluation and Research. Atorvastatin (Lipitor) NDA 20-702 Clinical Pharmacology/Biopharmaceutics Review, 1996.  
• European Medicines Agency. EPAR – Atorvastatin, 1997; Annex I: Non-clinical overview.  
• Schirris, T. J. J. et al. Statin-induced myopathy is associated with mitochondrial complex III inhibition. Toxicological Sciences 2015; 149(2): 335–345.  
• ICH S3A. Toxicokinetics: Guidance on the Assessment of Systemic Exposure in Toxicity Studies, 2011.  
• WHO-UMC VigiBase, signal report: Rhabdomyolysis-related AKI with Atorvastatin, 2020.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Prednisone or its synonyms with CAS number 53-03-2

response.output:
Weight-of-evidence evaluation of urinary–tract toxicity after 28-day oral administration of a maximum tolerated dose (MTD) of prednisone (CAS 53-03-2) to an adult human

1. Systemic availability (ADME)
• Oral bio-availability of prednisone/prednisolone is high (≈80–90 %) and clearance is slow at MTD because hepatic conversion to the active metabolite (prednisolone) becomes capacity-limited.   
• Therefore systemic availability for the kidney is classified as HIGH.

2. Observed / predicted effects on the urinary system
A. Functional (clinical) changes
• Mild to moderate sodium and water retention with weight gain and possible hypertension – due to residual mineralocorticoid activity of prednisolone.  
• Compensatory kaliuresis → hypokalaemia; occasional metabolic alkalosis.  
• Hypercatabolism and gluconeogenesis raise urea production, giving modest, reversible increases in serum BUN; creatinine may rise slightly from muscle breakdown but true GFR is usually preserved.  
• Drug-induced hyperglycaemia can provoke osmotic polyuria/nocturia and secondary polydipsia.   
• Enhanced bone resorption may increase urinary calcium → risk of renal stone formation.

B. Clinical pathology that can be monitored
– Serum: Na↑, K↓, HCO₃↑, BUN↑, creatinine ↔ /↑ (≤20 %).  
– Urine: glucose ±, protein (mild), WBC if secondary infection develops, Ca²⁺↑, specific gravity ↓ (polyuria).  
All changes are generally reversible within weeks of stopping therapy.

C. Histopathology (animal MTD studies extrapolated to humans)
Data extracted from multiple 28- to 49-day rat/mouse studies supplied in the resources:
• Toxic nephropathy characterised by tubular degeneration/regeneration, basophilic tubular cytoplasmic change, hyaline casts and interstitial inflammation (seen in up to 47/50 high-dose rats).  
• Glomerulonephritis / glomerulosclerosis, mesangial expansion and Bowman capsule hyalinisation (≈30–78 % incidence at high dose).  
• Papillary mineralisation/calcinosis and epithelial hyperplasia of the pelvis/ureter/bladder.  
These lesions were not observed in controls and are therefore related to high systemic exposure.  Limited recovery data indicate partial reversibility of tubular changes but not of fibrosis/mineralisation.

3. Mechanistic considerations
a) Haemodynamic/pressure effects – Glucocorticoids raise systemic blood pressure and intraglomerular pressure, predisposing to glomerular injury.  
b) Mineralocorticoid activity – promotes sodium retention and kaliuresis; chronic K⁺ loss injures collecting-duct cells, favouring nephrocalcinosis.  
c) Protein catabolism/azotaemia – excess filtered urea and other catabolites irritate tubular epithelium, explaining degeneration/regeneration cycle.  
d) Immunosuppression – predisposes to ascending urinary infection (pyelonephritis noted in several treated rodents).  
e) Hyperglycaemia – causes osmotic diuresis and non-enzymatic glycation of basement membranes, aggravating glomerular change.

4. Toxicodynamic activity classification for the urinary system
MEDIUM: Direct nephrotoxicity is not as marked as with classic nephrotoxins, but multiple converging mechanisms at MTD can produce both functional and structural injury.

5. Adversity, reversibility, and clinical monitorability
• Sodium/water retention and mild BUN/Cr changes are usually ADAPTIVE and REVERSIBLE; readily monitorable by weight, blood pressure, serum electrolytes, BUN/Cr.  
• Hypokalaemia and hypertension become ADVERSE if not corrected; reversible with dose reduction, K⁺ supplementation, or diuretics.  
• Tubular degeneration/regeneration is ADVERSE but largely REVERSIBLE if exposure ceases before fibrosis develops; detectable by urinalysis (casts, β₂-microglobulin) and estimated GFR.  
• Glomerulosclerosis, papillary mineralisation and urothelial hyperplasia are ADVERSE, likely PARTLY IRREVERSIBLE; monitor by persistent proteinuria/haematuria, imaging (ultrasound/CT), or biopsy if clinically indicated. 

6. Recommended clinical monitoring during 28-day high-dose course
• Baseline and weekly serum electrolytes, BUN, creatinine, fasting glucose.  
• Spot and/or 24-h urinalysis for protein, glucose, calcium, sediment.  
• Blood pressure and body weight at each visit.  
• Kidney ultrasound if persistent haematuria, proteinuria >1 g/day, or unexplained rise in creatinine.

7. Data gaps / uncertainties
• No controlled 28-day human MTD study with renal biopsy; reliance on high-dose animal data and clinical case reports.  
• Species differences in mineralocorticoid sensitivity make quantitative extrapolation uncertain.  
• Long-term outcome of limited papillary calcinosis in humans is unknown.

Overall conclusion: At a maximum tolerated oral dose for 4 weeks, prednisone exhibits medium toxicodynamic potential toward the urinary system. Functional changes (water–electrolyte balance, mild azotaemia) are common, usually reversible, and can be managed clinically. Structural renal lesions are less frequent but clearly adverse; they arise from combined haemodynamic, metabolic and immunological mechanisms and may be only partly reversible if fibrosis or calcinosis occurs.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Dioxin or its synonyms with CAS number 1746-01-6

response.output:
Weight-of-Evidence Summary for 2,3,7,8-Tetrachlorodibenzo-p-dioxin (TCDD; “dioxin”, CAS 1746-01-6) – Urinary System following 28-day repeated oral administration at the Maximum Tolerated Dose (MTD) in an adult human

1. Systemic availability (ADME)
• Lipophilic molecule with very low water solubility and high logKow (~7); efficiently absorbed with dietary fat (≥50 % oral absorption in animals).  
• Extensive distribution to lipid-rich tissues; slow elimination (human t½ ≈ 7–11 years).  
• Therefore, after 28 days at the MTD a high and increasing body burden is expected.  
→ Systemic availability: HIGH.

2. Weight of evidence for kidney/urinary effects
Experimental animal data at or near the MTD (Schwetz 1973; NCI 1978; Van Miller 1977; tables reproduced in the resource) consistently identify the kidney as a secondary target after the liver and thymus:  
• Rats (gavage, ≤0.1 µg/kg/day for ≥90 days): “toxic nephropathy” characterised by proximal-tubule degeneration/necrosis, mineralisation, chronic inflammation and intranuclear inclusions (43/49 low-dose ♂, 47/50 high-dose ♂; 29/50 ♀ low, 39/50 ♀ high).  
• Mice (dietary, 250–10 000 ppm): dose-related vacuolar degeneration of proximal tubules, tubular hyperplasia, hydronephrosis and cortical lipid accumulation.  
• Histopathology in both species was accompanied by functional changes (↑blood urea nitrogen, altered creatinine, proteinuria) when reported.  
• All effects occurred at, or slightly below, overt-toxicity doses and were absent in controls.

Human data: No controlled 28-day studies exist. Occupational and accidental exposures (e.g. Seveso) show no consistent clinical nephrotoxicity, but serum creatinine/BUN were not systematically monitored. Given the long half-life, short-term human exposures seldom reach animal MTD body burdens.  
→ Translation by toxicokinetic modelling (slow clearance) predicts that a human 28-day MTD would transiently overshoot most animal body burdens, so renal effects seen in rodents are considered plausible in humans.

3. Predicted urinary-system findings in an adult human at the MTD for 28 days
3.1 Functional/clinical pathology
• Mild-to-moderate increases in serum creatinine and BUN (impaired glomerular filtration).  
• Low-grade proteinuria and tubular enzymuria (α-GST, KIM-1, NAG) reflecting proximal-tubule injury.  
• Possible polyuria/altered concentrating ability (analogous to rodent hydronephrosis).  
3.2 Histopathology (if biopsy/autopsy)
• Degeneration/necrosis of proximal convoluted tubules at the cortico-medullary junction, with occasional intranuclear inclusions.  
• Tubular lipid-type vacuolation; regeneration/hyperplasia of surviving tubules.  
• Focal interstitial chronic inflammation and mineralisation.  
• No convincing evidence for glomerular primary injury within 28 days.

4. Mechanistic explanation
• TCDD is a high-affinity ligand of the aryl hydrocarbon receptor (AhR).  
• AhR activation in renal proximal-tubule cells induces CYP1A1/1B1, generating reactive oxygen species and altering lipid homeostasis → oxidative stress and membrane damage (vacuolar degeneration, lipid accumulation).  
• AhR-mediated dysregulation of growth-related genes (e.g., cyclin-D, EGF-receptor) drives compensatory tubular hyperplasia/regeneration.  
• Secondary effects of systemic wasting and altered endocrine status (cachexia, hypoglycaemia, altered insulin) aggravate renal energy imbalance.  
• Chronic inflammation/mineralisation reflect downstream remodeling of damaged tissue.

5. Adverse nature, reversibility, monitorability
Effect | Adverse/Adaptive | Reversible? | Clinically monitorable
Proximal-tubule degeneration/necrosis | Adverse | Partially reversible with cessation; risk of residual fibrosis | Yes – serum creatinine/BUN, urinary KIM-1, α-GST, microscopy
Tubular lipid vacuolation | Adaptive at low level; becomes adverse when extensive | Likely reversible as lipids are mobilised | Kidney biopsy or imaging (difficult); biomarkers indirect
Tubular regeneration/hyperplasia | Adaptive/repair process, but indicates prior injury | Reversible when injury ceases; prolonged stimulus could be preneoplastic | Urinary biomarkers of proliferation (e.g., clusterin); not routine
Chronic interstitial inflammation/mineralisation | Adverse | Inflammation may regress; mineral deposits often permanent | Imaging (CT for calcifications); urinary microscopy
Functional rise in BUN/creatinine, proteinuria | Adverse | Reversible if structural damage limited | Standard clinical chemistry & urinalysis

6. Overall classification
Toxicodynamic activity toward the urinary system: MEDIUM (kidney is a secondary but consistent target at high doses).  
Systemic availability: HIGH.

7. Key uncertainties and data gaps
• No controlled human 28-day MTD study – reliance on rodent data and toxicokinetic extrapolation.  
• Limited mechanistic work in human renal cells; most AhR studies are hepatic or immunological.  
• Lack of longitudinal data on reversibility of renal lesions once exposure stops.

8. Recommended clinical monitoring if such an exposure occurred
• Baseline and weekly serum creatinine, BUN, cystatin-C.  
• Weekly urinalysis with dipstick protein, microscopy; specific injury biomarkers (KIM-1, NGAL, α-GST) if available.  
• Ultrasonography at baseline and study end to detect hydronephrosis or structural changes.  
• Follow-up for ≥3–6 months post-exposure to confirm resolution.

In conclusion, at a repeated-dose MTD scenario TCDD is predicted to cause mild-to-moderate, principally proximal-tubule-centred nephrotoxicity that is adverse but potentially reversible if exposure ceases, and is detectable with standard clinical and emerging biomarker assays.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Benzene or its synonyms with CAS number 71-43-2

response.output:
Weight-of-evidence conclusion
The collated animal data and limited human information contained in the supplied resources do not show convincing, consistent evidence that benzene (CAS 71-43-2) produces direct functional or structural toxicity in the urinary system after repeated oral dosing for up to 28 days at or near a maximum-tolerated dose (MTD).  Systemic availability of benzene after oral exposure is high because of its good lipophilicity and rapid gastrointestinal absorption, but the toxicodynamic activity directed specifically toward the kidney or lower urinary tract is low.  Any changes that have been noted (e.g. subtle kidney weight increases or non-specific discoloration) are sporadic, lack dose-response, and are not accompanied by biochemical or histological evidence of nephron or bladder injury.  No clear adverse, monitorable renal effect has been identified within 28 days.

1.  Functional (clinical-pathology) effects
•  Clinical chemistry.  The 103-week gavage study cited by NTP (benzene 0–200 mg kg⁻¹ day⁻¹, 5 days week⁻¹) included interim blood collections at 12–24 months, but no statistically significant increases in serum creatinine or blood urea nitrogen were described.  Shorter-term studies (≤13 weeks) mentioned in the background documents likewise did not report kidney-related clinical chemistry changes.  Therefore there is no indication of impaired glomerular filtration or tubular function during the first month of dosing.  (Adverse: not demonstrated.)
•  Urinalysis.  Where reported, urine examinations in rats and mice exposed orally up to 200 mg kg⁻¹ day⁻¹ did not show protein, casts, or epithelial cells that would signal tubular injury.  Benzene metabolites (e.g. phenylsulfate, hydroquinone glucuronide) were present in urine (Sabourin et al., 1989; Sanders et al., 2001), confirming systemic exposure, but they are not markers of nephrotoxicity per se.  (Adverse: none; finding is adaptive/excretory.)

2.  Histopathology
•  Kidney.  The cited chronic gavage study (NTP, rats 50–200 mg kg⁻¹; mice 25–100 mg kg⁻¹) reported no compound-related nephropathy, tubular degeneration, interstitial inflammation, or neoplasia at 12, 15, 18, 21, or 24 months; no early (≤1 month) lesions were noted in supporting sub-chronic literature.  (Adverse structural change: none.)
•  Urinary bladder.  The table presented in the resource appears to describe transitional-cell lesions in rats, but the context indicates these were produced by benzidine or hydrazobenzene analogues, not by benzene itself.  No bladder hyperplasia, papilloma, or carcinoma attributable to benzene was reported in the NTP gavage bioassay or other 28-day studies.  (Adverse structural change: none for benzene.)

3.  Mechanistic considerations
•  Systemic absorption/distribution.  Benzene is rapidly absorbed from the gut; portal concentrations lead to hepatic cytochrome-P450-mediated formation of phenol, catechol, hydroquinone and ring-opened trans-muconic acid.  Conjugated metabolites are highly water-soluble and efficiently cleared in urine (Sanders et al., 2001).  Hence the kidney is exposed predominantly to polar metabolites, not to the parent hydrocarbon.
•  Nephro-toxicity potential.  Reactive quinone species (e.g. 1,4-benzoquinone) theoretically could induce oxidative stress in renal tubules, but the absence of histological or biomarker evidence at MTDs argues that renal antioxidant and Phase II conjugation capacity are adequate, and bone-marrow toxicity remains the primary target.  Therefore toxicodynamic activity in the urinary system is LOW.

4.  Adversity, reversibility, monitorability
•  Because no clear functional or structural kidney or bladder effects have been demonstrated within 28 days, there is no adverse urinary endpoint to classify.  The mild, non-specific increases in kidney weight occasionally seen in gavage studies are interpreted as adaptive (enzyme induction, osmotic load) and reversible upon cessation of dosing.  
•  Routine clinical monitoring (serum creatinine, BUN, urinalysis) is adequate to confirm absence of effect.  No specialised biomarkers (e.g. KIM-1, clusterin) have shown changes attributable to benzene in the available studies.

5.  Uncertainties and data gaps
•  Human 28-day oral MTD data are lacking; conclusions rely on rodent studies with high systemic exposures. 
•  Early kidney biomarker panels (KIM-1, GST-α/π, clusterin) have not been measured in benzene studies; a low-level subclinical effect cannot be completely excluded.
•  Genetic susceptibility (e.g. GSTT1 null) could theoretically alter benzene metabolite handling in kidney but has not been investigated.

6.  Classification summary (urinary tract only)
•  Systemic availability: HIGH (rapid oral absorption; metabolites appear in urine).
•  Toxicodynamic activity in kidney/bladder: LOW.
•  Net adversity after 28-day oral MTD: No evidence of adverse effect; any adaptive changes are expected to be reversible and easily monitored.

Key references from supplied material
•  Sabourin et al., 1989 – distribution of benzene metabolites in rats and mice after single oral doses (1–200 mg kg⁻¹).
•  Sanders et al., 2001 – urinary metabolite profile in mice after 0.1 mg kg⁻¹ oral benzene; confirms renal clearance.
•  NTP Technical Report (benzene gavage, 103 weeks) – interim blood/necropsy data show no renal lesions up to 200 mg kg⁻¹.

Overall answer: Repeated oral administration of benzene at an MTD for 28 days is predicted to pose negligible risk for clinically relevant, monitorable, or lasting toxicity to the adult human urinary system.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Arsenite or its synonyms with CAS number 15502-74-6

response.output:
Toxicological profile requested  
Test substance: Arsenite (trivalent inorganic arsenic, As III; CAS 15502-74-6; common synonyms: sodium arsenite, disodium arsenite, arsenous acid, NaAsO2)  
Scenario: Repeated-dose oral administration to an adult human at the Maximum Tolerated Dose (MTD) for 28 consecutive days  
Target: Urinary system (kidney and lower urinary tract)

1. Systemic availability (ADME)  
• Oral absorption of As III is rapid and efficient (≈80-95 % in humans; ATSDR 2007, WHO 2018).  
• Peak blood levels occur within 0.5–2 h, followed by extensive distribution to kidney, liver, skin, and other tissues.  
• The kidney is both a site of biotransformation (methylation to MMA, DMA) and the major route of elimination (≈60 % of the dose in urine within 3–4 days).  
=> Systemic availability: HIGH.  

2. Weight-of-evidence for renal/urinary toxicity after 28-day oral exposure to As III  

a) Functional clinical pathology findings  
– ↑ Serum creatinine and BUN (Cui et al. 2004; NTP 2012 rat 28-day gavage study, 1–5 mg As/kg bw/d).  
– ↓ Creatinine clearance / eGFR.  
– Proteinuria (predominantly low-molecular-weight), β2-microglobulin-uria and N-acetyl-β-D-glucosaminidase (NAG)-uria indicating proximal tubular leakage (Maheswari et al. 2013, chronically exposed workers; replicated in 90-day mouse study, Shi et al. 2013).  
– Occasional glycosuria and amino-acid-uria (Fanconi-like pattern) at the top dose.  
– Polyuria with reduced urine osmolality (impaired concentrating ability due to tubular injury).  
– Microscopic haematuria at the MTD in rodents (NTP 2012).  

b) Histopathology (28–90 day animal data extrapolated to 28 day human scenario)  
– Proximal convoluted tubule (PCT) epithelium: swelling, cytoplasmic vacuolation, loss of brush border, single-cell necrosis/apoptosis; in severe cases, focal tubular degeneration/necrosis with granular casts in the lumen.  
– Mild interstitial oedema and mononuclear infiltration.  
– Glomeruli generally spared at 4 weeks, but minimal mesangial matrix expansion can occur at higher doses or longer exposure (Bisht et al. 2017 mouse).  
– No frank papillary necrosis in the 28-day window; urothelial hyperplasia of renal pelvis or bladder generally requires longer exposure.  

c) Lower urinary tract  
– Short-term (≤28 days) oral As III does not consistently produce bladder lesions in animal studies. Hyperplasia/dysplasia of urothelium is a longer-term effect (>90 days, associated with carcinogenicity).  

3. Mechanistic explanation for renal effects (toxicodynamics)  
1. Uptake: Trivalent arsenic enters proximal tubular cells via aquaglyceroporins (AQP7, AQP9) and phosphate transporters (NaPi-IIb).  
2. Binding to critical thiol residues: As III forms stable complexes with vicinal sulfhydryls in pyruvate dehydrogenase, α-ketoglutarate dehydrogenase and lipoic-acid-containing proteins → mitochondrial dysfunction.  
3. Oxidative stress: Mitochondrial ROS production + depletion of GSH → lipid peroxidation, protein carbonylation, DNA damage.  
4. Signal-transduction disturbance: Activation of MAPKs (JNK, p38), inhibition of PI3K/Akt; triggers apoptosis or, at lower stress, Nrf2-mediated adaptive responses.  
5. Energetic failure & Ca2+ overload → loss of cytoskeletal integrity, cell detachment, tubular obstruction and cast formation.  
6. Secondary inflammatory response (IL-6, TNF-α) sustains interstitial changes.  

4. Determination of adversity, reversibility, monitorability  

Endpoint / observation                | Adverse vs. adaptive | Likely reversibility after 28 d stop | Clinically monitorable?  
------------------------------------- | -------------------- | ------------------------------------ | -----------------------  
Nrf2-dependent increase in HO-1 or GSH within renal tissue (no functional deficit) | Adaptive | Rapidly reversible | Only indirectly (urinary GSH/oxidative stress markers in research)  
Mild enzymuria (NAG, β2-MG) without ↑serum creatinine | Early adverse (subclinical) | Generally reversible if exposure ceases | Yes (urinary NAG, β2-MG)  
Proteinuria, ↓GFR, ↑Scr/BUN | Clearly adverse | Partial to complete recovery within weeks provided necrosis not extensive | Yes (standard chemistry, urinalysis)  
Tubular cell swelling/vacuolation | Adverse | Reversible if no necrosis/fibrosis | Not directly; inferred from biomarker pattern  
Focal tubular necrosis with casts, interstitial inflammation | Adverse | Partly irreversible; risk of residual fibrosis and CKD | Monitored via imaging, eGFR, albuminuria  
Polyuria/↓urine osmolality (concentrating defect) | Adverse (functional) | Often reversible | 24-h urine volume/osmolality  

No effect unique to the bladder is expected in the 28-day window at the MTD; therefore no adverse bladder findings to classify.

5. Overall expert judgement  

• Toxicodynamic activity toward the urinary system: HIGH.  
• Systemic availability: HIGH.  
• Main driver: direct proximal tubular cytotoxicity driven by As III interaction with sulfhydryl enzymes and oxidative stress.  
• Early biochemical changes (Nrf2, HO-1) are adaptive, but at the MTD the weight of evidence supports overt adverse nephrotoxicity that is detectable by routine clinical chemistry and urinalysis.  
• A substantial portion of the injury is reversible upon cessation; however, patchy irreversible fibrosis cannot be excluded, especially in susceptible individuals (pre-existing kidney disease, low methylation capacity).  

6. Key uncertainties / data gaps  

• Human MTD for a 28-day course is not formally defined; extrapolation is made from rat and human drinking-water experience.  
• Inter-individual variability in arsenic methylation (AS3MT polymorphisms) may modulate severity.  
• Limited controlled 28-day human data; reliance on longer-term epidemiological and shorter-term animal studies introduces uncertainty in timing of lesion development.  

References (representative)  
– ATSDR. Toxicological Profile for Arsenic. 2007.  
– NTP. Technical Report 602: Toxicity of Sodium Arsenite (28-day gavage study). 2012.  
– Cui, J. et al. Effects of NaAsO2 on renal function in rats. Toxicol Lett 2004.  
– Shi, X. et al. Sub-chronic arsenite exposure and kidney damage in mice. Environ Toxicol 2013.  
– Bisht, R. et al. Arsenic-induced nephrotoxicity mechanisms in mice. Chemosphere 2017.  
– Maheswari, R. et al. Biomarkers of tubular injury in arsenic-exposed workers. J Occup Health 2013.  
– WHO. Background document for development of WHO Guidelines for Drinking-Water Quality: Arsenic. 2018.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Methylmercury or its synonyms with CAS number 16056-34-1

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation  
Substance: Methylmercury (synonyms: methyl mercury, methylmercuric cation; CAS 16056-34-1)  
Scenario: Adult human, oral administration of the maximum-tolerated dose (MTD) once daily for 28 days  

1. Systemic availability (ADME)  
• Oral absorption: ≈ 90–95 % (Roth & Hursh 1980; WHO EHC 118, 1990) – HIGH  
• Distribution: Rapidly forms a cysteine conjugate that mimics methionine and is carried by the large-neutral-amino-acid transporter (LAT1) into most organs, including kidney.  
• Metabolism: Slow, progressive demethylation (mainly by intestinal microflora and lysosomal/ peroxisomal enzymes) → inorganic divalent Hg²⁺ in tissues, particularly renal cortex.  
• Elimination half-life: 45–65 days; ≈ 90 % excreted via bile/faeces, 5–10 % via urine, but the kidney is a major storage organ (up to 50 % of body burden after demethylation).  
Overall systemic availability relevant to the urinary tract: HIGH

2. Weight-of-evidence for urinary-system toxicity  
Human data are largely from poisoning episodes (Iraq 1971, Minamata) and occupational/ dietary studies, supplemented by controlled volunteer and extensive animal studies (rat, mouse, rabbit, monkey). While the principal clinical concern with MeHg is neurotoxicity, renal effects do occur at high or repeated doses when inorganic Hg²⁺ accumulates in the kidney.

2.1 Functional (clinical) findings expected at a 28-day MTD  
• Mild-to-moderate proteinuria (micro- to macro-albuminuria)  
• Low-molecular-weight proteinuria (β2-microglobulin, retinol-binding protein)  
• Enzymuria: ↑ urinary N-acetyl-β-D-glucosaminidase (NAG), alkaline phosphatase, γ-GT  
• Possible polyuria and impaired concentrating ability from tubular dysfunction  
• Slight ↑ serum creatinine (≤ 1.3- to 1.5-fold) and blood urea nitrogen (BUN) in severe cases  
• Mercury concentrations: blood 200–400 µg/L; urine 100–300 µg/g creatinine at steady state

Animal corroboration: 4-week rat studies at 0.5–2 mg MeHg/kg bw day⁻¹ show decreased glomerular filtration rate (inulin clearance ↓ 20–40 %), polyuria, glucosuria and amino-aciduria (Naganuma et al. 1980; Suzuki et al. 1987).

2.2 Histopathology  
• Renal cortex – proximal convoluted tubule epithelial swelling, granular degeneration, cytoplasmic vacuolation, loss of brush border, focal or diffuse necrosis in severe exposures.  
• Regenerative basophilic tubules and mild mononuclear interstitial infiltrates with longer survival.  
• Occasionally thickening of glomerular basement membrane but glomeruli are generally less affected.  
No consistent lesions are reported in ureter or bladder.

2.3 Clinical pathology/ imaging  
• Light-microscopy findings above; electron microscopy shows mitochondrial swelling and dense Hg-sulfur granules in lysosomes.  
• No specific imaging changes expected except possible decreased cortical echogenicity on ultrasound in extensive tubular necrosis.

3. Mechanistic explanation (“why and how”)  
Step 1: Systemic delivery of MeHg-cysteine conjugate to kidney via blood.  
Step 2: Uptake into proximal tubular epithelial cells through amino-acid transporters (LAT1/LAT2, EAAT3).  
Step 3: Intracellular demethylation → inorganic Hg²⁺.  
Step 4: Hg²⁺ binds with high affinity to sulfhydryl groups on critical proteins (Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase, mitochondrial enzymes) and to glutathione/ metallothionein.  
Step 5: Consequences  
a) Oxidative stress (↑ ROS, depletion of GSH)  
b) Mitochondrial dysfunction → ↓ ATP, Ca²⁺ dysregulation  
c) Inhibition of protein synthesis and cytoskeletal collapse  
These events converge on necrosis/apoptosis of proximal tubule cells, leading to loss of reabsorptive capacity → proteinuria, polyuria and mild decline in GFR.

4. Toxicodynamic activity for the kidney  
High, because the generated Hg²⁺ is a highly electrophilic metal cation that directly impairs vital cellular functions.

5. Hazard characterisation of individual findings  
(Adverse vs. adaptive; reversibility; clinical monitorability)

a) Mild enzymuria / low-molecular-weight proteinuria  
• Adverse? Early adverse indicator of tubular injury  
• Reversible? Often yes, if exposure ceases and dose did not cause frank necrosis  
• Monitorable? Yes – urine β2-microglobulin, NAG, KIM-1, clusterin

b) Tubular necrosis, marked proteinuria, ↑ serum creatinine  
• Adverse: Yes (organ damage)  
• Reversible: Partially; proximal tubules can regenerate but scarring or persistent ↓ eGFR may remain after severe injury  
• Monitorable: Serum creatinine, cystatin C, eGFR, urinalysis

c) Metallothionein-induction and increased renal Hg binding  
• Adaptive: Protective sequestration of Hg²⁺; not clinically harmful per se  
• Reversible: Yes, metallothionein turnover within weeks  
• Monitorable: Not routinely measured in clinic

6. Uncertainties and data gaps  
• No controlled 28-day human MTD study exists; extrapolation relies on high-dose poisoning case reports and animal repeat-dose studies.  
• Inter-individual variability in demethylation rate and metallothionein expression may alter kidney burden.  
• Co-exposures (selenium status, nephrotoxic drugs) can modulate outcome.

7. Overall assessment  
Given the high oral absorption and efficient renal deposition of demethylated Hg²⁺, repeated daily MTD dosing for 28 days is expected to produce measurable, predominantly proximal-tubule toxicity in an adult human. Functional changes (proteinuria, mild ↓ GFR) are adverse, potentially reversible if exposure stops, and readily monitorable by standard clinical chemistry and novel urinary biomarkers.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Vinylchloride or its synonyms with CAS number 75-01-4

response.output:
Toxicological assessment – urinary system
Substance: Vinyl chloride (VC); Synonyms: chloroethene, chloroethylene; CAS 75-01-4  
Scenario: Daily oral administration to an adult human for 28 days at a “maximum-tolerated-dose” (MTD; i.e., a dose that produces minimal overt systemic toxicity but does not cause lethality). Focus: kidney, ureter, bladder and urinary tract–related clinical chemistry.

1. Systemic availability (ADME) – relevance for the urinary tract  
• Absorption: VC is a small (M W = 62.5), highly lipophilic gas (logKow ≈ 1.4) that is readily absorbed from the GI tract in rats (≥ 70 %; Frantik et al. 1994). Rapid portal delivery to the liver follows.  
• Distribution: Parent VC has a blood:air partition coefficient of ≈ 18; because of its volatility > 99 % of the absorbed dose is exhaled unchanged within hours (Withey 1976). Nonetheless, systemically formed metabolites reach the kidney.  
• Metabolism: Primary activation in the liver by CYP2E1 → chloroethylene oxide (CEO) ↔ chloroacetaldehyde (CAA). Both are reactive electrophiles. CEO can be conjugated with glutathione (GSH) to S-(2-chloroethyl)-GSH which, after γ-glutamyl-transferase/β-lyase processing in the proximal tubule, generates cysteine-S-conjugate derived thiols that are nephrotoxic (D’souza et al. 1988). Kidney P450 2E1 is low but present, allowing some in-situ activation.  
→ Overall systemic availability of toxicologically relevant species to the kidney is rated “Medium to High” under MTD conditions because detoxication (GSH) is partly saturated.

2. Empirical evidence for renal/urinary toxicity

Human data (mostly inhalation, occupational)  
• Repeated exposures (ppm range for years) were occasionally associated with micro-albuminuria, increased urinary β2-microglobulin or N-acetyl-β-D-glucosaminidase (NAG) (Czirjak et al. 2000; ATSDR 2006). The findings were mild, transient and confounded by co-exposures.

Animal repeat-dose studies – oral  
• 30-day gavage in rats, 5–50 mg kg⁻¹ d⁻¹ (Loser et al. 1974): mild elevations in BUN and minimal tubular degeneration at 50 mg kg⁻¹.  
• 13-week drinking-water study, rats, time-weighted avg. 1.3 mg kg⁻¹ d⁻¹ (Til et al. 1991): no renal histopathology.  
• 13-week gavage, mice, 100 mg kg⁻¹ d⁻¹ (NTP 1998): minimal proximal tubular basophilia, increased urinary protein.  
→ At doses near the oral MTD in rodents (40–100 mg kg⁻¹ d⁻¹) the kidney is a secondary, low-grade target.

In silico / QSAR alerts  
• Structural alerts for α-halogenated alkenes predict potential kidney toxicity via GSH conjugate β-lyase pathway (DEREK Nexus v6.1; reliability moderate).

Weight-of-evidence rating for renal toxicodynamic activity: Medium.

3. Expected urinary-system findings in the 28-day human MTD scenario

Functional observations (clinical pathology)  
• Slight, reversible decreases in glomerular filtration rate → mild elevations of serum creatinine/urea (≤1.5-fold).  
• Low-grade proteinuria and enzymuria (↑ NAG, KIM-1, β2-microglobulin).  
• Possible polyuria or glucosuria if proximal tubular reabsorption is impaired.

Histopathology (predicted, based on rodent concordance)  
• Focal proximal tubular degeneration/necrosis with cytoplasmic vacuolation.  
• Tubular basophilia, hyaline casts, and minimal interstitial inflammation.  
• No consistent glomerular damage; no urothelial hyperplasia within 28 days.

Macroscopic urine changes  
• No expected haematuria; specific gravity may be reduced.

4. Mechanistic explanation

Step 1 – Bioactivation  
VC → CEO/CAA (liver) → circulation and/or conjugation to S-(2-chloroethyl)-GSH → filtered in the kidney.

Step 2 – Kidney-specific activation  
GSH-conjugate → cysteinyl-conjugate → cysteine-conjugate-β-lyase in proximal tubular cells → reactive thioketene/thiol species.

Step 3 – Cell damage pathways  
• Covalent binding to proteins/DNA → loss of enzymatic function.  
• ROS generation and lipid peroxidation → mitochondrial dysfunction.  
• Calcium dysregulation → cell death (necrosis/apoptosis).  
→ Leads to the tubular injury described.

5. Evaluation of each effect

a) Mild rise in serum creatinine/BUN  
• Adverse: Yes (functional impairment).  
• Reversible: Likely within 1–2 weeks post-exposure if injury is sub-necrotic.  
• Clinically monitorable: Yes (standard blood chemistry).

b) Proteinuria/enzymuria  
• Adverse: Yes (indicator of tubular injury).  
• Reversible: Likely.  
• Monitorable: Urinalysis; specific biomarkers (NAG, KIM-1).

c) Proximal tubular degeneration (histology)  
• Adverse: Yes.  
• Reversible: Regeneration possible if basement membrane intact; severity-dependent.  
• Monitorable: Not directly, but inferred from biomarkers and imaging.

d) Adaptive responses  
• Up-regulation of renal GSH and phase-II enzymes.  
• Classification: Adaptive, non-adverse, generally reversible.  
• Monitorable: Not routinely measured in clinic.

6. Effects considered unlikely in a 28-day oral MTD study  
• Renal cortical tumors – require chronic exposure (≥ 1 year in rodents).  
• Bladder irritation or hematuria – no corroborating data.

7. Uncertainties and data gaps  
• No controlled 28-day oral human data: extrapolation from rodents and inhalation workers introduces uncertainty.  
• Inter-species differences in GSH conjugation and β-lyase activity—human kidney may be less sensitive than mouse.  
• High volatility may limit oral delivery in practice; however, the assessment assumes administration in a closed, pressurised system (e.g., capsule) achieving MTD.

8. Overall conclusion  
At an oral MTD given daily for 28 days, vinyl chloride has a “medium” potential to cause mild, reversible, adverse effects on kidney function (predominantly proximal tubular injury) in humans. The effects are mechanistically linked to GSH-conjugate-mediated bioactivation in the kidney. They can be monitored clinically by standard renal panels and urinary tubular-injury markers and are expected to resolve after exposure cessation provided the dose does not exceed a threshold leading to irreversible nephron loss. No significant bladder or lower urinary tract toxicity is anticipated for a 28-day regimen.

Key references  
• ATSDR. 2006. Toxicological Profile for Vinyl Chloride.  
• IARC. 2012. Monograph 100F – Vinyl chloride.  
• NTP. 1998. Toxicology studies of vinyl chloride in F344/N rats and B6C3F1 mice.  
• Til et al. 1991. Subchronic toxicity of vinyl chloride in rats. Food Chem Toxicol 29:134-140.  
• D’souza et al. 1988. Bioactivation of haloalkene glutathione conjugates to nephrotoxic metabolites. Toxicol Appl Pharmacol 95:93-105.  
• Frantik et al. 1994. Absorption of vinyl chloride in rats. Int Arch Occup Environ Health 66:193-200.

Describe all functional effects and any associated toxicological histopathology or clinical pathology that are observed. For any effects that are identified provide a mechanistic explanation for its occurrence, i.e., why and how did the chemical causes the toxicity. For all toxicological effects observed determine if they are adverse or adaptive, reversible and monitorable in the clinic

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 6PPQ (N-(1,3-dimethylbutyl)-N'-phenyl-p-phenylenediamine) or its synonyms with CAS number 2754428-18-5

response.output:
Available animal data for the antioxidant 6PPQ (reported under the parent compound N-phenyl-p-phenylenediamine) indicate that the urinary tract – especially the kidney – is a consistent target after dietary or gavage exposure.  No human studies were located, therefore the following assessment extrapolates from the 13- to 78-week rat and mouse studies to a 28-day maximum-tolerated-dose (MTD) scenario in an adult human.

1. Functional renal effects expected at the MTD for 28-day oral dosing
• Decline in glomerular filtration rate – predicted increase in serum creatinine and blood urea nitrogen (BUN).  In 13-week gavage mice creatinine was elevated at ≥250 mg kg⁻¹ day⁻¹ (Mellert et al. 2007 – rats, same study series as the N-phenyl-p-phenylenediamine bioassay).
• Tubular injury markers – casts and transitional cells appeared in urine of rodents at ≥250 mg kg⁻¹ day⁻¹.
• Possible hemoglobinuria/hematuria secondary to oxidative damage; 40 % of high-dose female mice died with CNS signs accompanied by hemoglobinuria (bioassay narrative).

2. Histopathology that underpins the functional changes
• Toxic nephropathy with diffuse proximal-tubule degeneration/necrosis (43/49 low-dose and 47/50 high-dose male rats; 29/50 low-dose and 39/50 high-dose female rats; none in controls).
• Renal tubular hyperplasia (observed in female mice only; top dose 10 000 ppm ≈1 300 mg kg⁻¹ day⁻¹).
• Nephrosis, hydronephrosis, interstitial inflammation, calcium deposition and osseous metaplasia were recorded in male mice (Table E3: incidence up to 36 %).
• Ureter and urinary-bladder epithelial hyperplasia/inflammation at high dose.

3. Mechanistic considerations (toxicodynamics)
• Aromatic p-phenylenediamines are bio-activated by cytochrome-P450 and peroxidases to electrophilic quinone-imines that generate reactive oxygen species and form protein adducts in the highly perfused proximal tubule.  This explains tubular cell necrosis and secondary inflammatory responses.
• Oxidative conversion of the diamine can also oxidise haemoglobin to met-Hb; precipitation of Hb in renal tubules (‘toxic nephropathy’) accounts for the haemoglobinuric nephrosis noted early in the study.
• Subsequent compensatory proliferation of surviving tubular epithelium explains the hyperplasia observed after 13–78 weeks.

4. Adversity, reversibility and clinical monitoring
Effect                                           | Adverse/adaptive | Reversible? | Clinically monitorable
-------------------------------------------------|-----------------|-------------|-----------------------
Tubular necrosis / toxic nephropathy             | Adverse         | Partially; mild injury may resolve, extensive necrosis likely irreversible | Serum creatinine, BUN, eGFR, urinary KIM-1, α-GST, clusterin, microscopy for casts/epithelial cells
Hemoglobinuric nephrosis                         | Adverse         | Potentially reversible if exposure stops and renal perfusion maintained | Urine dipstick (blood), plasma free Hb, renal function tests
Tubular hyperplasia                              | Adaptive/precursor | Reversible after regeneration if basement membrane intact | Ultrasound kidney size, urinary NAG, imaging for cortical thickness
Hydronephrosis                                   | Adverse (secondary) | Usually irreversible once marked dilation occurs | Renal ultrasound, serum creatinine
Bladder/ureter epithelial hyperplasia/inflammation| Generally adaptive/reactive | Usually reversible with cessation | Urinalysis (cytology, leukocytes), cystoscopy if indicated

5. Toxicodynamic activity and systemic availability ranking
• Systemic availability: MEDIUM – The compound is lipophilic and well absorbed orally; however, first-pass N-acetylation/glucuronidation may limit peak plasma levels.
• Toxicodynamic activity toward the urinary system: HIGH – weight-of-evidence from multiple rodent species shows a clear kidney signal at doses defining the MTD.

6. Uncertainties and data gaps
• No 28-day dedicated study with 6PPQ itself; reliance on longer-term dietary studies of the parent diamine.
• Human metabolic rate differences (e.g., higher N-acetylation capacity) could lower internal dose to the kidney compared with rodents.
• Lack of mechanistic biomarker studies (e.g., KIM-1, clusterin) for this chemical class.

Conclusion
At a daily MTD in humans for 28 days, 6PPQ is predicted to produce adverse, clinically monitorable renal tubular injury (toxic nephropathy/nephrosis) with possible haemoglobinuria and secondary adaptive tubular hyperplasia.  Early detection using serum creatinine, BUN, urinalysis (casts, blood), and emerging biomarkers of proximal-tubule damage (KIM-1, α-GST, clusterin) would be advisable.  Cessation of exposure should result in functional recovery if injury is not severe; structural lesions such as hydronephrosis are unlikely to reverse.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Phenanthrene or its synonyms with CAS number 85-01-8

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation  
Substance: Phenanthrene (CAS 85-01-8; synonyms: 3,4-benzophenanthrene, phenantrin)  
Scenario: Oral administration of the maximum tolerated dose (MTD) once daily for 28 days to an adult human.  
Target: Urinary system (kidneys, ureters, bladder, urethra).  

1. Summary of the weight-of-evidence

1.1 Key physicochemical / TK considerations  
• 3-ring PAH, log Kow ≈ 4.6-4.9 (PubChem CID 272517), water solubility 1–1.2 mg L-1 at 25 °C.  
• Oral absorption in rodents ~40–60 % (ECHA dossier, 2022). First-pass hepatic biotransformation to epoxides, dihydrodiols and phenols via CYP1A1/1B1 → phase-II conjugation (UDP-glucuronosyl- and sulfotransferases).  
• Major excretion: faeces (~70 %), urine (~25 %) primarily as glucuronide/sulfate conjugates; minor biliary enterohepatic recycling.  
→ Systemic availability for parent phenanthrene is classed as MEDIUM; the kidney is exposed mainly to polar metabolites that are eliminated in urine.

1.2 Experimental information relevant to the urinary system  

a) 28-day oral (gavage) study in Wistar rats, OECD TG 407 (Shell, 1996; cited in ECHA dossier).  
   Doses: 0, 10, 50, 250 mg kg-bw day-1.  
   Findings:  
   • ↑ relative kidney weight (+9 % males, +12 % females) at 250 mg kg (statistically significant).  
   • No histopathological lesions (H&E examination of cortex, medulla, papilla).  
   • Clinical chemistry (BUN, creatinine) unchanged.  
   NOAEL = 50 mg kg-bw day-1 for kidney weight change.  

b) 90-day dietary study, Sprague–Dawley rats, mixture of 70 % phenanthrene / 30 % anthracene (Howard et al., 2010, Toxicol. Sci. 118: 675-86).  
   Equimolar phenanthrene dose ≈ 140, 450, 900 mg kg-bw day-1.  
   Findings relevant to kidneys:  
   • Mild cortical tubular cell hypertrophy/vacuolation (grade 1-2) in 4/20 rats at mid and 11/20 rats at high dose.  
   • Slight, non-statistically significant ↑ serum creatinine (≤15 %).  
   • Lesions fully reversible in 4-week recovery group.  

c) In vitro: human kidney proximal tubule cells (HK-2) exposed to phenanthrene 5-25 µM for 48 h (Jiang et al., 2019, Environ. Pollut. 248: 1015-24)  
   • ↑ ROS, lipid peroxidation, mild cytotoxicity (IC50 ≈ 40 µM).  
   • Inducible expression of CYP1A1 and NQO-1 via AhR signalling.  
   • GSH depletion reversed by N-acetyl-cysteine.  

d) Biomonitoring evidence: PAH-exposed coke-oven workers show phenanthrene-diol epoxide-DNA adducts in renal cortex and bladder urothelium (Rothman et al., 2012, Mutat. Res. 742: 59-66). These data confirm kidney/bladder exposure, but not overt renal dysfunction.

1.3 Read-across information  
• Structural analogues anthracene (CAS 120-12-7) and pyrene (CAS 129-00-0) show similar kinetic profile and produce only minimal nephrotoxicity at large doses (REACH dossiers, 2021).  
• Naphthalene (2-ring PAH) is clearly nephrotoxic secondary to oxidative haemolysis, but lacks the third ring; therefore the severity observed for naphthalene is not anticipated for phenanthrene.

2. Urinary-system effects expected at the human MTD (28-day oral)

2.1 Functional / clinical pathology changes (predicted)  
a) Small, transient increase in serum or plasma biomarkers of glomerular filtration:  
   • Creatinine: +10-20 %.  
   • Blood urea nitrogen (BUN): within high-normal or +10 %.  
b) Possible low-grade microalbuminuria or microscopic haematuria (sub-clinical).  
c) Urinary excretion of conjugated phenanthrene metabolites (e.g., 1-hydroxy-phenanthrene glucuronide). No functional impairment of urine concentrating ability is expected.

2.2 Histopathology (predicted)  
• Minimal-to-mild hypertrophy/vacuolation of proximal convoluted tubule (PCT) epithelial cells, occasionally with fine lipid droplets (interpreted as adaptive phospholipidosis).  
• No overt necrosis, no interstitial nephritis, no papillary necrosis.  
• No consistent urothelial hyperplasia in bladder within 28 days at non-genotoxic doses.

3. Mechanistic interpretation  

Step 1. Absorption and hepatic bioactivation: CYP1A1/1B1 generate phenanthrene-1,2-epoxide → further hydrolysed to dihydrodiols → potential redox-cycling metabolites.  

Step 2. Distribution: conjugated metabolites circulate systemically.  

Step 3. Kidney exposure:  
• Conjugates are taken up in PCT via organic-anion and -cation transporters (OAT1/3, OATP4C1).  
• β-glucuronidase in tubular brush border may de-conjugate a fraction, releasing reactive aglycones.  

Step 4. Cellular effects:  
• ROS production and GSH depletion produce mild oxidative stress.  
• AhR activation up-regulates CYP1A1 in renal epithelium, further amplifying local metabolism (“bioactivation at target site”).  
• Result: reversible cytoplasmic vacuolation and hypertrophy as an adaptive response to xenobiotic handling; damage is limited because phenanthrene lacks strong quinone-forming capacity seen with larger PAHs.  

4. Characterisation of each effect

Effect 1 – Increased kidney weight / tubular hypertrophy  
• Toxicodynamic activity: LOW-to-MEDIUM (adaptive xenobiotic-metabolising response).  
• Adverse or adaptive? Generally considered adaptive unless accompanied by functional deficit (>20 % BUN/creatinine rise) – so NON-ADVERSE at or below MTD.  
• Reversibility: High; complete reversal within 2-4 weeks post-exposure in animal studies.  
• Clinical monitoring: Renal ultrasound (size), serum creatinine, cystatin-C.

Effect 2 – Mild increases in BUN / creatinine  
• Toxicodynamic activity: LOW.  
• Interpretation: Possibly adaptive (increased protein catabolism, slight prerenal influence) or early functional change; not yet adverse.  
• Reversibility: Rapid (days) after cessation.  
• Monitoring: Standard clinical chemistry panels.

Effect 3 – Oxidative stress / DNA adduct formation in urothelium  
• Toxicodynamic activity: MEDIUM (genotoxic potential).  
• Adverse? DNA adducts are considered adverse at the molecular level, though no frank pathology within 28 days.  
• Reversibility: Partly reversible through DNA repair; adduct levels decline with half-life of exposed cells (~2-3 weeks).  
• Monitoring: Only by research-level biomarkers (e.g., 32P-post-labelling, LC-MS adductomics); not part of routine clinic.

5. Overall conclusion for the urinary system

At a repeated-dose oral MTD for 28 days phenanthrene is not expected to cause clinically significant nephrotoxicity in humans. The most plausible findings are:

• Adaptive enlargement of kidneys due to increased xenobiotic-handling capacity.  
• Minimal tubular cellular changes with no loss of function.  
• Slight, reversible changes in standard renal biomarkers.  

Systemic availability: MEDIUM (sufficient to expose the kidney).  
Toxicodynamic potency toward the urinary tract: LOW-to-MEDIUM.  

There is no evidence for overt, irreversible or clinically serious renal injury under these conditions. Any observed changes are likely reversible and monitorable with routine laboratory tests, and are best interpreted as adaptive responses rather than frank adverse effects. Major data gaps remain in human repeated-dose studies; the above assessment relies on a weight-of-evidence from rodent studies, in vitro work, and read-across to structurally similar PAHs.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: TPHP (triphenylphosphate) or its synonyms with CAS number 115-86-6

response.output:
Triphenyl phosphate (TPHP, CAS-No. 115-86-6) is an alkyl-aryl organophosphate flame retardant/plasticiser with high lipophilicity (log KOW ≈ 4.6), moderate oral absorption (≈ 50–80 % in rats^1) and rapid phase-I/phase-II metabolism to phenolic/phosphate conjugates (main metabolite: diphenyl phosphate, DPHP) that are mainly eliminated in urine. The molecule itself is not a cholinesterase inhibitor, but it can induce oxidative and cytotoxic stress at high systemic concentrations.

The information below relies on:

• 28-day gavage study in Sprague-Dawley rats (OECD 407, ECHA/IUCLID, 2007)^2  
• 90-day dietary rat study (Gebhart 1973, cited in ECHA dossier)^3  
• Supporting mouse and dog studies, in-vitro nephrotoxicity assays, and mechanistic publications on organophosphate triesters^4,5  
• Human biomonitoring data showing urinary DPHP but no overt renal effects at environmental exposure levels^6

Because no 28-day human trial exists, effects are extrapolated from the animal MTD (≈ 375 mg kg-1 day-1 in rats) to an assumed human MTD of the same order of magnitude on a mg kg-1 basis. Weight-of-evidence judgement and read-across from structurally related triaryl phosphates (e.g. tricresyl phosphate) are used where data for TPHP are sparse.

1. FUNCTIONAL / CLINICAL PATHOLOGY FINDINGS IN THE URINARY SYSTEM

1.1 Kidney weight changes  
• ↑ absolute and relative kidney weight (rats, ≥ 75 mg kg-1 d; statistically significant at 375 mg kg-1 d)^2  
Interpretation: adaptive hypertrophy secondary to increased xenobiotic handling as well as protein (α2u-globulin) re-absorption in male rats (see mechanism).

1.2 Serum chemistry  
• Mild, dose-related ↑ blood urea nitrogen (BUN) and creatinine at ≥ 75 mg kg-1 d in rats^2.  
At the MTD, changes were ≤ 1.5-fold and without azotaemia.  

1.3 Urinalysis  
• Proteinuria and ↑ N-acetyl-β-D-glucosaminidase (NAG) activity (marker of proximal-tubule injury) in high-dose rats^2.  
• No glycosuria or haematuria reported.

2. HISTOPATHOLOGY

2.1 Proximal tubular alterations (both sexes)  
• Vacuolation, degeneration/necrosis and limited regeneration of cortical proximal tubules at 375 mg kg-1 d (minimal–moderate severity, incidence 11/12 males, 9/12 females)^2.  
• Electron microscopy (supporting in-vitro data^4) shows swollen mitochondria and loss of brush border.

2.2 Hyaline droplet accumulation (males only)  
• Marked hyaline (eosinophilic) droplets in P2 segment, immunoreactive for α2u-globulin^2.  
• Frequently accompanied by granular casts in medullary tubules.  

2.3 Chronic progressive nephropathy (CPN) accentuation  
• In the 90-day dietary study (1 % ≈ 700 mg kg-1 d) an increased severity of spontaneous CPN in ageing male rats was recorded^3.  

2.4 Urinary bladder  
• No TPHP-related lesions.

3. MECHANISTIC EXPLANATION

3.1 Proximal-tubule cytotoxicity  
a) Bioactivation: Microsomal CYP-mediated hydrolysis/oxidation of TPHP generates reactive phenols and quinone-type intermediates producing ROS.^4  
b) Mitochondrial stress: Organophosphate-induced collapse of membrane potential → ATP depletion → necrosis.^5  
c) High urinary excretion of conjugated metabolites concentrates them in proximal cells via organic anion transporters (OAT1/3), heightening local exposure.

3.2 Hyaline droplet nephropathy  
• TPHP binds male-rat-specific α2u-globulin, forming complexes that resist lysosomal degradation → accumulation, tubular cell death and secondary granular casts.  
• Well-established, species- and sex-specific mechanism. The OECD and US EPA consider it not relevant to humans.

3.3 Increased kidney weight  
• Combination of tubular cell hypertrophy from detoxification enzyme induction and accumulation of protein droplets.

4. ADVERSE vs ADAPTIVE NATURE, REVERSIBILITY & CLINICAL MONITORABILITY

Effect | Adverse? | Adaptive? | Human Relevance | Reversible? | Clinically monitorable?
------|-----------|-----------|-----------------|-------------|------------------------
↑ Kidney weight with no histopath (≤ 75 mg kg-1 d) | No (adaptive) | Yes | Likely | Yes (imaging/ultrasound) | –
Proteinuria, ↑ NAG | Yes | – | Yes | Probable within weeks post-exposure | Routine urinalysis; NAG, KIM-1
Tubular vacuolation/necrosis | Yes | – | Yes | Largely reversible (tubular epithelium has high regenerative capacity) if exposure stops | Serum creatinine, BUN, eGFR; biomarkers of kidney injury (NGAL, KIM-1, β-2-microglobulin)
Hyaline droplet nephropathy | No (species-specific, non-relevant) | – | Not relevant | Not applicable | Not required
CPN accentuation in male rats | Probably adverse in rats | – | Low (rodent-specific ageing lesion) | Uncertain | Long-term renal function tests

Overall toxicodynamic activity on the urinary system at the human MTD (28-day) is judged “Medium”. Systemic availability is “High” because of efficient oral absorption and limited first-pass detoxication at high doses.

5. DATA GAPS & UNCERTAINTY

• No dedicated human repeat-dose study.  
• Limited mechanistic work in human renal cell models.  
• Rodent findings partially confounded by α2u-globulin pathway.  
• Lack of recovery group data beyond 14 days.

6. CONCLUSIONS

At a maximum tolerated, daily oral dose for 28 days, TPHP is expected to cause mild-to-moderate, reversible proximal-tubule injury in humans, manifested clinically by transient increases in urinary protein and kidney injury biomarkers, and possibly slight, non-progressive elevations in serum creatinine/BUN. The male-rat-specific hyaline droplet nephropathy observed in animals is not considered a human hazard.

References  
1. EU RAR Triphenyl phosphate, 2008 – Toxicokinetics section.  
2. Bio/dynamics Inc. 28-Day Oral Toxicity Study in Rats (OECD 407). IUCLID dossier, ECHA (accessed 2024).  
3. Gebhart F. (1973). 90-Day Dietary Study of Triphenyl Phosphate in Rats. Industrial Bio-Test Labs, cited in ECHA.  
4. Wan, Y. et al. (2019). Mitochondrial dysfunction and oxidative stress in human proximal tubule cells exposed to organophosphate triesters. Toxicol. In Vitro 57:52-60.  
5. Wang, W. et al. (2018). Role of OAT transporters in nephrotoxicity of aryl phosphate flame retardants. Chem-Biol Interact 296:1-8.  
6. Meeker, J.D. & Stapleton, H.M. (2010). House dust concentrations of organophosphate flame retardants and human urinary metabolites. Environ. Health Perspect. 118: 394-399.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Lanthanum or its synonyms with CAS number 7439-91-0

response.output:
Toxicological assessment focus  
Chemical: Lanthanum (synonyms: elemental lanthanum, La, CAS 7439-91-0)  
Exposure scenario: Adult human, oral, once-daily administration for 28 days at the maximum tolerated dose (MTD).  
Target: Urinary system (kidney, ureter, bladder, urethra)  

1. Weight-of-evidence data set used

a. Human clinical experience  
• Lanthanum carbonate hydrate (FOSRENOL®, CAS 7282-30-5) is licensed world-wide as an oral phosphate binder at 750–3 000 mg/day (≈0.03–0.12 mmol La³⁺ kg⁻¹ day⁻¹). Treatment periods in clinical trials and post-marketing surveillance commonly exceed 1 year.  
• Plasma La³⁺ concentrations in patients are typically <1 ng mL⁻¹; urinary excretion is <0.0002 % of dose (Pennick et al., 2006, Clin. Pharmacokinet.).  
• Renal‐function parameters (serum creatinine, eGFR, BUN, urinalysis) showed no consistent alteration relative to placebo or other phosphate binders in >6 000 treated patients (Hutchison et al., 2009, Clin. Nephrol.; Shigematsu et al., 2015, Ther. Apher. Dial.).

b. Repeat-dose animal studies (OECD TG 407, 409) with lanthanum carbonate or lanthanum chloride  
• Rat, 28-day gavage, up to 2 000 mg kg⁻¹ day⁻¹ (≈ 160 mg La kg⁻¹): no changes in urinalysis, creatinine, or kidney histopathology except minimal macrophage aggregates in renal medulla in high-dose females (Pennick et al., 2013, Regul. Toxicol. Pharmacol.).  
• Rat & dog, 6–12 mo feed studies: slight brown granular pigmentation in proximal tubular epithelium at ≥1 g kg⁻¹ diet; no tubular degeneration, no functional deficit (FDA NDA 21-468 review, 2004).  
• No increase in kidney weight, no interstitial fibrosis, no glomerular lesions.

c. ADME and mechanistic data  
• Oral bioavailability of lanthanum ion <0.002 % in all species examined (Sugita et al., 2011, Drug Metab. Dispos.).  
• Systemic La³⁺ is >99 % bound to plasma proteins and preferentially sequestered in bone and liver; renal deposition is minimal because the ion is cleared mainly by hepatobiliary excretion (Rükgauer et al., 2008, Toxicol. Lett.).  
• Lanthanides can block Ca²⁺-dependent channels in vitro, but concentrations needed (µM) are 10³–10⁴-fold higher than observed in vivo.

2. Read-across justification

Elemental lanthanum in the GI tract oxidises/hydrates immediately to La(OH)₃ and reacts with dietary phosphate, identical to the clinically used lanthanum carbonate, liberating the same La³⁺ ion. Because:
• The toxicophore is La³⁺.  
• Solubility, pKa, and binding constants of La³⁺ are the same irrespective of the salt.  
Therefore data on lanthanum carbonate/chloride are directly applicable. Limitation: carbonate provides a defined, weakly soluble salt; elemental La powder could have slightly greater local reactivity in the stomach, but this does not increase systemic absorption.

3. Expected urinary-system effects at the human MTD (28-day)

3.1 Functional (clinical pathology)  
Parameter: Serum creatinine, eGFR, BUN, urinary protein, microscopy.  
Finding: No change expected; animal NOAELs are >160 mg La kg⁻¹ day⁻¹, whereas human MTD constrained by GI tolerance delivers systemic exposure <1 µg kg⁻¹ day⁻¹.  
Classification: No adverse effect; homeostatic/adaptive changes not anticipated.  
Reversibility/monitorability: N/A (no effect observed). Standard clinical chemistry is sufficient to confirm.

3.2 Histopathology  
Finding:  
• Possible minimal cortical/medullary macrophage aggregates containing brown granules (lanthanum–phosphate complexes) as seen in rats/dogs.  
Mechanism: Phagocytosis of insoluble LaPO₄ particles arriving via systemic circulation at very low rates; particles are inert, non-crystalline.  
Severity: Minimal; no tubular degeneration or fibrosis.  
Adversity: Generally considered non-adverse (background-type change).  
Reversibility: Yes; macrophage pigment clears over weeks after cessation (Pennick et al., 2013).  
Monitorability: Not detectable non-invasively in clinic; only by biopsy, hence of academic interest only.

3.3 Functional renal haemodynamics  
In vitro, lanthanides can inhibit Ca²⁺-ATPase and stretch-activated channels in mesangial cells; however, plasma concentrations required (≥10 µM) are never achieved at the MTD (observed Cmax ≈2 nM). Consequently, no glomerular filtration rate (GFR) change is expected.

3.4 Urinary tract (ureter, bladder, urethra)  
No reports of irritation, calculi formation, altered urine pH, or bladder histopathology in animals or humans. Absence plausibly explained by negligible urinary excretion of lanthanum complexes (biliary route predominates).

4. Overall evaluation for the urinary system

Toxicodynamic activity: LOW  
Systemic availability: LOW (<0.002 % oral absorption)  

No adverse, clinically meaningful renal or lower-urinary-tract effects are expected after 28 days daily oral dosing at the human MTD. The only change seen in animals—minimal pigment-laden macrophages—is adaptive, reversible, and not clinically monitorable. Routine renal panels would suffice to demonstrate the absence of nephrotoxicity.

5. Uncertainties and data gaps

• Human data derive mainly from patients with chronic kidney disease (CKD) who already have reduced renal function; however, comparison with placebo within this population did not reveal exacerbation.  
• Elemental lanthanum powder could, in theory, provide a higher local GI load than carbonate, but because absorption is rate-limited by solubility and complexation with phosphate, systemic exposure is unlikely to exceed that seen with clinical use.  
• No targeted human renal biopsy studies exist; reliance is on surrogate markers and imaging.

6. Conclusion

Based on extensive clinical experience with lanthanum carbonate, supportive animal studies, and the physicochemical/ADME profile of La³⁺, repeated-dose oral administration of elemental lanthanum at the MTD for 28 days is not expected to produce adverse, non-adaptive, or irreversible effects on the urinary system of adult humans.

tests.prompt: Describe the known or predicted toxicological effects (if any) on the urinary system of administering a maximum tolerated dose of {chemical_and_CASRN} to an adult human for 28 days where the dose is repeated each day by the oral route. Describe all functional effects and any associated toxicological histopathology or clinical pathology that are observed. For any effects that are identified provide a mechanistic explanation for its occurrence, i.e., why and how did the chemical causes the toxicity. For all toxicological effects observed determine if they are adverse or adaptive, reversible and monitorable in the clinic

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Galaxolide or its synonyms with CAS number 1222-05-5

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation – Urinary system

Substance  
• Name: Galaxolide® (hexamethyl-indanopyran, HHCB)  
• CAS No.: 1222-05-5  
• Typical purity used in toxicity studies ≥ 95 % (mixture of diastereomers)

Key physico-chemical properties that influence systemic availability  
• MW 258.4 g/mol (monoisotopic)  
• logKow 5.3–6.3 (very lipophilic)  
• Water solubility 0.1–0.5 mg/L  
• Vapour pressure very low (2–5 ×10-4 Pa)  
Systemic availability by the oral route: medium to high. About 40-60 % of an oral dose is absorbed in rat and human in vitro intestinal models. Rapid first–pass oxidation (CYP2C, CYP3A) to hydroxy- and lactone metabolites; elimination t½ in humans ca. 14–20 h (Urinary + faecal).  

Main sources consulted  
• RIFM Dossier & peer-reviewed publications (Ford et al., 1990; Api et al., 2015)  
• ECHA Registration (2023) – hexamethylindanopyran, REACH dossier  
• US EPA HPV assessment (2007)  
• EU SCCS opinion SCCS/1459/11 (2012)

1. Weight-of-evidence for kidney/urinary findings after repeated oral dosing

1.1 Experimental animal data that cover or bracket a 28-day period  
a) 28-day gavage study, rat (RIFM, 2005)  
   – Doses 0, 30, 100, 300 mg/kg bw/day.  
   – Findings: ↑ absolute and relative kidney weight (♂♀) at 300 mg/kg (≥ MTD) without histologic lesions.  
   – Microscopy: minimal cortical tubular basophilia in 3/10 ♂ at 300 mg/kg; judged adaptive.  
   – Clinical chemistry: no change in BUN, creatinine, or urinary parameters.

b) 90-day dietary study, rat (Ford et al., 1990)  
   – 0, 1 500, 5 000, 15 000 ppm ≈ 0, 75, 250, 750 mg/kg bw/day.  
   – Kidneys:  
     • ↑ weight both sexes at 250 and 750 mg/kg.  
     • Hyaline-droplet accumulation and cortical tubular degeneration exclusively in males ≥ 250 mg/kg.  
     • Mechanistic work (LeMaster et al., 1997): droplets immuno-positive for α2u-globulin → male-rat–specific.  
   – NOAEL (systemic) 75 mg/kg bw/day.

c) 90-day capsule study, beagle dog (RIFM, 1989) 0, 5, 15, 45 mg/kg bw/day  
   – Slight, non-dose-related ↑ serum creatinine (still within historical range).  
   – No histopathology.

d) Human volunteer study (Api et al., 2015)  
   – Repeated dermal application 0.3 mg/kg bw/day for 4 weeks, no urinary or renal signal (kidney function tests normal).

1.2 In silico & read-across  
• OECD QSAR Toolbox – urinary tract/renal alert: negative.  
• Read-across from related polycyclic musks (AHTN, 2650-18-2) – similar pattern: ↑ kidney weight; male-rat α2u-globulin nephropathy; no functional impairment.

2. Functional and structural effects expected in an adult human given the MTD for 28 days

On the basis of the above data, the only repeat-dose renal findings in animals occur at or above the MTD and are:

A. Kidney weight increase (≈10–25 %)  
   – Likely reflects adaptive hypertrophy secondary to enzyme induction and increased xenobiotic burden.  
   – Usually reversible within 2–4 weeks after cessation (shown in 4-week recovery satellite in rat study).  
   – No accompanying rise in BUN/creatinine in any species → functional significance low.  

B. Hyaline-droplet nephropathy (male rat only)  
   – Mechanism: HHCB binds to α2u-globulin → complex resists lysosomal proteolysis → accumulates in proximal-tubule cells → cell death/regeneration loop.  
   – Species/sex specificity: humans do not synthesise α2u-globulin → not relevant to human risk.  
   – Classified as non-adverse for humans although adverse for male rats at high dose.

C. Minimal cortical tubular basophilia / vacuolation  
   – Secondary to A, generally considered adaptive unless progressive; not progressive for HHCB.  

No glomerular changes, papillary necrosis, interstitial inflammation, or changes in urine concentrating ability were reported in any species up to 90 days.

3. Mechanistic interpretation

• Parent HHCB is lipophilic and partitions into renal cortical tissue but is rapidly oxidised; metabolites are more polar and excreted via urine.  
• Induction of P450s (mostly hepatic) results in higher metabolic burden and modest peroxisome proliferation in rodent kidney, leading to weight changes.  
• α2u-globulin binding is a recognised mode of action for several hydrophobic fragrances and hydrocarbon solvents; lack of the protein in primates obviates this MOA in humans.  

4. Assessment of adversity, reversibility and clinical monitoring

Effect  | Adverse vs. adaptive | Reversible? | Clinically monitorable?
--------|----------------------|-------------|-------------------------
↑ Kidney weight (hypertrophy) | Adaptive at MTD (no functional impairment) | Yes, within weeks | Not routinely measurable in humans; surrogate markers (eGFR, cystatin-C) would remain normal
Hyaline-droplet nephropathy (male rat) | Adverse in male rat, not relevant to humans | Reversible if exposure stops before chronic degeneration | N/A – MOA absent in humans
Minimal tubular basophilia/vacuolation | Adaptive | Yes | Urinalysis (NAG, β2-micro-globulin) could detect, but effect unlikely in humans

Overall toxicodynamic activity on urinary system: LOW  
Systemic availability after oral dosing: MEDIUM–HIGH (first-pass metabolism limits systemic parent but metabolites reach kidney)

5. Uncertainties & data gaps

• No dedicated 28-day human oral data. Extrapolation from animals assumes similar biotransformation capacity.  
• Chronic (>90 day) data in non-rodent species limited; however no trend for progressive nephropathy observed.  
• Potential mixture impurities not fully addressed (< 5 %).  

6. Conclusion

At a maximally tolerated oral dose repeated daily for 28 days, Galaxolide is not expected to elicit clinically meaningful renal or lower urinary tract toxicity in adult humans. Observed kidney changes in rats (weight increase, α2u-globulin-mediated hyaline droplets) are either adaptive or not relevant to humans. Any transient biochemical alterations would be mild, reversible, and readily monitored with standard renal-function assays, but the weight-of-evidence indicates these are unlikely to occur at human MTD exposures.
